literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5–7); Beatitudes; a string of kingdom-character descriptions.
speaker: Jesus
summary: The merciful are blessed — they will receive mercy in return.
interpretive_notes: Mercy is reciprocal and eschatological; beatitude form implies that mercy is a defining kingdom character trait.

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5–7); section on exceeding the law; paired with love of enemies (5:43–44).
category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
speaker: Jesus
summary: Give to everyone who begs; do not refuse the borrower.
interpretive_notes: No means test or qualifying condition; generosity to beggars and borrowers is absolute.

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5–7); sixth antithesis; Jesus radicalises the love command beyond natural limits.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Love your enemies and pray for persecutors — kingdom love has no limits.
interpretive_notes: Extends neighbor-love to enemies; mirrors divine indiscriminate generosity (v.45).

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 6); triad of piety — almsgiving, prayer, fasting; Jesus assumes giving to the poor is a regular practice.
category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
speaker: Jesus
summary: Give to the needy in secret, not for public praise — the Father who sees in secret will reward.
interpretive_notes: Jesus assumes almsgiving as standard disciple practice; the command is about motive and manner, not whether to give.

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 6); Lord's Prayer; economic language used as moral metaphor for forgiveness.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Forgive our debts as we forgive our debtors — divine forgiveness conditioned on human mercy.
interpretive_notes: Debt language draws on Jubilee and Sabbath-year economic ethics; forgiveness of moral and economic debts are intertwined.

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 6); teaching on single-minded devotion; followed immediately by the lamp of the body and serving two masters.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Do not hoard earthly treasure — store treasure in heaven instead.
interpretive_notes: Kingdom inversion of economic priorities; hoarding wealth is directly counter-commanded.

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 6); concludes the section on treasure and anxiety; absolute statement on divided loyalty.
speaker: Jesus
summary: You cannot serve both God and money — loyalty is binary.
interpretive_notes: Absolute statement with no qualifications; wealth-service is incompatible with God-service.

literary_context: Jesus eating with tax collectors at Matthew's house (Matt 9:9–13); Pharisees question his table fellowship; Jesus quotes Hosea 6:6.
speaker: Jesus
summary: God desires mercy, not sacrifice — Jesus defines his mission through prophetic mercy-priority.
interpretive_notes: Hosea 6:6 quoted twice in Matthew; mercy-practice ranks above cultic obedience.

literary_context: Mission discourse (Matt 10); Jesus commissions the Twelve for their first mission; mercy acts are central to the commission.
category: A13 — Heal the sick / free the oppressed
speaker: Jesus
summary: Heal the sick, raise the dead, cleanse lepers — you received freely, give freely.
interpretive_notes: Mercy acts are the content of the disciples' mission; healing is given without economic barrier.

literary_context: Response to John the Baptist's question from prison (Matt 11:2–6); Jesus lists the evidences of his messianic identity.
category: A13 — Heal the sick / free the oppressed
speaker: Jesus
summary: Good news to the poor is listed as a messianic credential alongside healing and resurrection.
interpretive_notes: The poor receive good news last in the list — climactic position; quoting Isa 61:1 as self-description.

literary_context: Following condemnation of unrepentant cities (Matt 11:20–24); Jesus offers relief to those crushed by religious and economic burden.
category: A11 — Bear one another's burdens / help the weak
speaker: Jesus
summary: Come to me, all who are weary and burdened — I will give you rest.
interpretive_notes: Addressed to those crushed by labor and legal burden; Jesus offers rest as an act of mercy.

literary_context: Sabbath controversy over plucking grain (Matt 12:1–8); Pharisees accuse disciples; Jesus quotes Hosea 6:6 a second time.
speaker: Jesus
summary: If you understood mercy you would not condemn the innocent — mercy outranks ceremonial law.
interpretive_notes: Second quotation of Hos 6:6 in Matthew; reinforces mercy as the interpretive key to the law.

literary_context: Feeding of the 5,000 (Matt 14:13–21); Jesus withdraws after John's death; crowds follow; compassion precedes both healing and feeding.
category: A13 — Heal the sick / free the oppressed
speaker: Jesus / Narrator
summary: Jesus had compassion on the crowd and healed their sick.
interpretive_notes: Splanchnizomai — visceral, gut-level compassion; always drives action in Matthew; context leads immediately to the feeding command.

literary_context: Feeding of the 5,000 (Matt 14:13–21); disciples suggest sending crowds away; Jesus redirects the obligation back to the disciples.
category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
speaker: Jesus
summary: You give them something to eat — the disciples are commanded to feed the crowd.
interpretive_notes: Imperative placed on disciples, not Jesus alone; disciples are agents of mercy, not bystanders. Parallel instance across all three Synoptics.

literary_context: Feeding of the 4,000 (Matt 15:29–39); second feeding miracle; Jesus explicitly states compassion as the motive.
category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
speaker: Jesus
summary: Jesus has compassion on the hungry crowd — they have been with him three days with nothing to eat.
interpretive_notes: Second feeding miracle; Jesus' stated motivation is physical hunger, not spiritual need alone.

literary_context: Community discourse (Matt 18); Peter asks about forgiveness limits; Jesus answers with a number and a parable of a king who cancels an enormous debt.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Forgive not seven times but seventy-seven — the debt parable illustrates mercy without limit.
interpretive_notes: Forgiveness is framed in economic terms (canceled debt); the servant who refuses mercy loses his own; structural argument for unlimited forgiveness.

literary_context: Rich young ruler encounter (Matt 19:16–30); man asks what he must do; Jesus answers the final barrier is wealth.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Sell your possessions and give to the poor — then come follow me.
interpretive_notes: Wealth is the specific barrier to this man's discipleship; the command is not general but it follows a general principle repeatedly stated.

literary_context: Request of James and John's mother (Matt 20:20–28); disciples dispute greatness; Jesus redefines it as service.
category: A12 — Servant leadership / serve rather than rule
speaker: Jesus
summary: Whoever wants to be great must be servant — the Son of Man came to serve, not to be served.
interpretive_notes: Greatness is structurally redefined as service; Jesus grounds the command in his own identity and mission.

literary_context: Greatest commandment dialogue (Matt 22:34–40); Pharisee lawyer tests Jesus; Jesus summarizes the whole law in two commands.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Love your neighbor as yourself — the second great commandment, like the first.
interpretive_notes: All NT ethics flows from this; repeated across Gospels and Epistles more than any other single command.

literary_context: Seven woes against scribes and Pharisees (Matt 23); Jesus critiques selective obedience that honors tithing while neglecting the moral core of the law.
category: A6 — Pursue economic equality / oppose exploitation
speaker: Jesus
summary: You neglect justice, mercy, and faithfulness — the weightier matters of the law.
interpretive_notes: Jesus explicitly ranks mercy and justice as heavier than tithing; a direct hierarchy within the law.

literary_context: Parable of the sheep and goats (Matt 25:31–46); eschatological judgment scene; criteria of judgment are acts of mercy to the vulnerable.
category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
speaker: Jesus
summary: I was hungry and you fed me, thirsty and you gave me drink — mercy to the vulnerable is mercy to Christ.
interpretive_notes: Six mercy categories are listed as the criteria of final judgment; no doctrinal test is mentioned alongside them.

literary_context: Parable of the sheep and goats (Matt 25:31–46); the king identifies himself with the suffering poor.
category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
speaker: Jesus
summary: Whatever you did for the least of these you did for me — Christ identifies with the vulnerable.
interpretive_notes: Christological grounding of mercy: the basis of obligation is Christ's own identity with the poor and suffering.

literary_context: Parable of the sheep and goats (Matt 25:31–46); the negative verdict — those who failed to show mercy are separated from the king.
category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
speaker: Jesus
summary: Failure to act mercifully toward the vulnerable is equivalent to failing Christ himself.
interpretive_notes: Neglect of mercy is not passive — it results in active judgment. The test is exclusively mercy-based.

literary_context: Feeding of the 5,000 (Mark 6:30–44); disciples return from mission; crowds gather; late in the day; disciples suggest dismissal.
category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
speaker: Jesus
summary: Parallel to Matt 14:16 — disciples are commanded to feed the hungry crowd.
interpretive_notes: Same Parallel Instance ID as Matt 14:16; not primary record. Mark's account is the earliest.

literary_context: Feeding of the 4,000 (Mark 8:1–10); second feeding miracle; Jesus initiates with explicit statement of compassion for physical hunger.
category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
speaker: Jesus
summary: Parallel to Matt 15:32 — Jesus has compassion on the crowd's physical hunger.
interpretive_notes: Mark's account is probably primary; compassion for bodily need stated as the explicit motive.

literary_context: Rich young ruler (Mark 10:17–31); Jesus looks at the man and loves him before giving the hard command about wealth.
subtheme: Sell All and Give to Poor
speaker: Jesus
summary: Go, sell all you have and give to the poor — parallel to Matt 19:21.
interpretive_notes: Mark uniquely notes Jesus 'loved him' before issuing the command; wealth barrier is stated with tenderness.

literary_context: Request of James and John (Mark 10:35–45); disciples dispute greatness; Jesus responds with a structural redefinition.
category: A12 — Servant leadership / serve rather than rule
speaker: Jesus
summary: Parallel to Matt 20:26-28 — greatness equals servanthood.
interpretive_notes: Mark's version includes 'slave of all' — the most radical formulation.

literary_context: Greatest commandment dialogue (Mark 12:28–34); a scribe asks sincerely; Jesus gives the two great commands; the scribe agrees that mercy exceeds sacrifice.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Love your neighbor as yourself — no commandment is greater than these.
interpretive_notes: Mark's scribe responds that this exceeds 'all burnt offerings and sacrifices' (v.33), echoing Hosea 6:6.

literary_context: Temple treasury (Mark 12:41–44); Jesus watches wealthy donors and a poor widow; he interprets the widow's gift as surpassing all others.
category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
speaker: Jesus / Narrator
summary: The poor widow gave more than all the rich — she gave everything she had to live on.
interpretive_notes: Generosity measured not by amount but by sacrifice; poverty + total giving = highest offering.

literary_context: Magnificat (Luke 1:46–55); Mary's song after visiting Elizabeth; programmatic declaration of kingdom reversals.
category: A6 — Pursue economic equality / oppose exploitation
speaker: Mary
summary: God fills the hungry with good things and sends the rich away empty.
interpretive_notes: Magnificat as prophetic frame for Luke's entire Gospel; the hungry/rich reversal is structural, not incidental.

literary_context: John the Baptist's ethical teaching (Luke 3:7–14); crowds ask what repentance looks like; John answers with concrete economic acts.
speaker: John the Baptist
summary: Whoever has two tunics, share with the one who has none — repentance produces economic redistribution.
interpretive_notes: Repentance ethics are immediately material; sharing clothing and food is the fruit of genuine turning.

literary_context: John the Baptist's ethical teaching (Luke 3:7–14); soldiers ask what repentance requires of those with coercive power.
category: A6 — Pursue economic equality / oppose exploitation
speaker: John the Baptist
gk_keywords: diaseiō (extort by shaking/threatening); sykophanteō (falsely accuse for gain)
summary: Do not extort or falsely accuse — be content with your wages.
interpretive_notes: Economic justice addressed specifically to those with power to exploit; extortion named as the repentance issue for soldiers.

literary_context: Nazareth synagogue (Luke 4:14–21); Jesus reads from Isaiah scroll; declares 'Today this scripture is fulfilled in your hearing' — programmatic mission statement.
category: A13 — Heal the sick / free the oppressed
speaker: Jesus
summary: Jesus is anointed to bring good news to the poor, liberty to captives, sight to the blind, freedom for the oppressed.
interpretive_notes: Programmatic statement of Jesus' entire ministry; poverty, captivity, disability, and oppression are each named as kingdom targets. Jubilee year language.

literary_context: Sermon on the Plain (Luke 6:17–49); Lukan parallel to Beatitudes; addressed directly to disciples — 'you who are poor' is literal.
category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
speaker: Jesus
summary: Blessed are you who are poor — the kingdom belongs to you.
interpretive_notes: Luke uses 'you who are poor' (literal) vs Matthew's 'poor in spirit'; both honor poverty as kingdom-significant.

literary_context: Sermon on the Plain (Luke 6:17–49); Beatitudes; literal physical hunger addressed.
category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
speaker: Jesus
summary: Blessed are you who are hungry now — you shall be satisfied.
interpretive_notes: Literal physical hunger; satisfaction is an eschatological promise that grounds present obligation.

literary_context: Sermon on the Plain (Luke 6:17–49); Lukan woes follow the beatitudes — a structural balance absent from Matthew.
category: A6 — Pursue economic equality / oppose exploitation
speaker: Jesus
summary: Woe to the rich — you have already received your comfort. Woe to the full — you shall be hungry.
interpretive_notes: Luke's woes are structural counterparts to the beatitudes; wealth and fullness are signs of misplaced comfort, not divine blessing.

literary_context: Sermon on the Plain (Luke 6:27–36); section on love of enemies and unconditional generosity.
category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
speaker: Jesus
summary: Give to everyone who begs — do not demand back what is taken.
interpretive_notes: Parallel to Matt 5:42; Luke's form may be slightly broader — 'everyone who asks.'

literary_context: Sermon on the Plain (Luke 6:27–36); section on love of enemies and lending; closes with the command to mirror the Father's mercy.
category: A5 — Lend without return / cancel debts
speaker: Jesus
summary: Lend expecting nothing in return — be merciful as your Father is merciful.
interpretive_notes: Generosity is modeled explicitly on divine mercy; economic lending placed alongside love of enemies.

literary_context: Sermon on the Plain (Luke 6:37–42); teaching on judgment and generosity — the measure you use will be used for you.
category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
speaker: Jesus
summary: Give and it will be given to you — pressed down, shaken together, running over.
interpretive_notes: Generosity operates on a divine reciprocal principle; the metaphor is agricultural measurement — grain overflowing a vessel.

literary_context: John the Baptist's question from prison (Luke 7:18–23); Jesus answers by pointing to his mercy acts as messianic credentials.
category: A13 — Heal the sick / free the oppressed
speaker: Jesus
summary: Parallel to Matt 11:5 — mercy acts and good news to the poor are Jesus' messianic evidence.
interpretive_notes: Climactic position of 'the poor have good news' signals its kingdom centrality.

literary_context: Feeding of the 5,000 (Luke 9:10–17); disciples suggest dismissing the crowd; Jesus redirects the obligation to them.
category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
speaker: Jesus
summary: Parallel to Matt 14:16 — You give them something to eat.
interpretive_notes: Third synoptic record of the same command; disciples are consistently cast as agents of feeding.

literary_context: Good Samaritan dialogue (Luke 10:25–37); a lawyer answers Jesus' counter-question correctly; Jesus affirms and says 'Do this and you will live.'
speaker: Lawyer / Jesus
summary: Love the Lord your God and love your neighbor as yourself — do this and you will live.
interpretive_notes: Luke's version spoken by the lawyer whom Jesus affirms; immediately followed by the Good Samaritan as the definition of 'neighbor.'

literary_context: Parable of the Good Samaritan (Luke 10:25–37); Jesus defines 'neighbor' through a story involving a despised outsider who crosses ethnic and religious lines to show mercy.
speaker: Jesus
summary: The Samaritan had compassion, crossed ethnic lines, and paid for full care — he is the neighbor.
interpretive_notes: Neighbor is defined by merciful action, not ethnic or religious proximity; Samaritan = paradigmatic mercy-giver despite social hostility.

literary_context: Woes against Pharisees (Luke 11:37–54); Jesus at a Pharisee's table; challenges their external purity focus.
category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
speaker: Jesus
summary: Give what is inside as alms — then everything is clean for you.
interpretive_notes: Almsgiving is the antidote to religious hypocrisy; inner generosity produces outer purity.

literary_context: Warning against greed (Luke 12:13–21); a man asks Jesus to settle an inheritance; Jesus refuses and warns the crowd about covetousness.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Guard against all covetousness — life does not consist in the abundance of possessions.
interpretive_notes: Greed named as a spiritual danger, not a social inconvenience; the parable of the rich fool follows.

literary_context: Teaching on anxiety and provision (Luke 12:22–34); addressed to disciples after the parable of the rich fool; closes with a command to sell and give.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Sell your possessions and give to the needy — store treasure in heaven.
interpretive_notes: This command is addressed to all disciples, not only the rich ruler; general discipleship standard.

literary_context: Teaching at a Pharisee's banquet (Luke 14:1–24); Jesus observes guests choosing places of honor; instructs the host on kingdom hospitality.
category: A10 — Include the marginalized / seat the outcast
speaker: Jesus
summary: Invite the poor, crippled, lame, and blind to your feasts — not those who can repay.
interpretive_notes: Kingdom hospitality is defined by inability to repay; the marginalised are to fill the place of social peers.

literary_context: Parable of the great banquet (Luke 14:15–24); invited guests refuse; host fills the table with the excluded.
category: A10 — Include the marginalized / seat the outcast
speaker: Jesus
summary: Bring in the poor, crippled, blind, and lame — fill the banquet hall.
interpretive_notes: God's kingdom fills its table with those the social order excludes; the excluded are the invited.

literary_context: Parable of the prodigal son (Luke 15:11–32); part of a triad of parables on the lost (lost sheep, lost coin, lost son); addressed to Pharisees criticising Jesus' table fellowship.
speaker: Jesus
summary: The father ran to his returning son, embraced him, and called for a feast — extravagant mercy.
interpretive_notes: God's mercy is modeled as undignified, running, pre-emptive welcome; the economic ruin of the son is the context.

literary_context: Parable of the rich man and Lazarus (Luke 16:19–31); addressed to Pharisees who loved money (v.14); permanent reversal of fortunes in the afterlife.
category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
speaker: Jesus
summary: Rich man feasts while Lazarus starves at his gate — after death the positions are permanently reversed.
interpretive_notes: Lazarus is the only named character in a parable; neglect of the poor at one's own gate has eternal consequence.

literary_context: Rich ruler (Luke 18:18–30); ruler claims full law-keeping; Jesus identifies wealth as the remaining barrier.
subtheme: Sell All and Distribute to Poor
speaker: Jesus
summary: Sell all you have and distribute to the poor — parallel to Matt 19:21 and Mark 10:21.
interpretive_notes: Third synoptic record; Luke uses 'distribute' (diadidōmi) — active redistribution language.

literary_context: Zacchaeus encounter (Luke 19:1–10); chief tax collector climbs a tree; Jesus invites himself over; Zacchaeus responds with restitution and generosity.
category: A6 — Pursue economic equality / oppose exploitation
speaker: Zacchaeus
summary: Zacchaeus gives half his goods to the poor and repays fourfold what he defrauded.
interpretive_notes: Conversion produces immediate economic reorientation; restitution (fourfold) exceeds legal requirement; Jesus declares 'salvation has come to this house.'

literary_context: Temple treasury (Luke 21:1–4); Jesus observes wealthy donors and a poor widow; interprets her gift.
category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
speaker: Jesus / Narrator
summary: Parallel to Mark 12:43-44 — the poor widow gave all she had to live on.
interpretive_notes: Luke's account is essentially identical to Mark's; sacrificial giving from poverty surpasses large gifts from abundance.

literary_context: Last Supper dispute over greatness (Luke 22:24–27); disciples argue about who is greatest; Jesus inverts the hierarchy.
category: A12 — Servant leadership / serve rather than rule
speaker: Jesus
summary: The greatest must become as the youngest — I am among you as the one who serves.
interpretive_notes: Luke's version set at the Last Supper; Jesus grounds servant leadership in his own table identity.

literary_context: Foot washing at the Last Supper (John 13:1–20); Jesus washes the disciples' feet; interprets it as a model of mutual service.
category: A12 — Servant leadership / serve rather than rule
speaker: Jesus
summary: As I have washed your feet, you ought to wash one another's feet — do as I have done.
interpretive_notes: Only in John; foot washing was a slave's task; command to replicate grounds servant ethics in Christological example.

literary_context: Last Supper discourse (John 13:31–35); immediately after Judas departs; Jesus gives the community its defining command.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Love one another as I have loved you — by this all will know you are my disciples.
interpretive_notes: The 'newness' is the Christological standard (as I have loved you); love of community is the church's public witness to the world.

literary_context: Farewell discourse (John 15:1–17); vine and branches; Jesus defines the commandment he expects his disciples to keep.
speaker: Jesus
summary: This is my commandment — love one another as I have loved you.
interpretive_notes: Parallel to John 13:34; here embedded in the vine/branches teaching; love-command is the content of abiding in Christ.

literary_context: Pentecost community summary (Acts 2:42–47); describes the economic life of the Jerusalem church immediately after the Spirit's coming.
speaker: Narrator
summary: All who believed had all things in common — selling possessions to distribute to any who had need.
interpretive_notes: Voluntary redistribution of wealth as the economic expression of Pentecost; 'as any had need' is need-based, not equal-distribution.

literary_context: Second Jerusalem community summary (Acts 4:32–37); follows the apostles' prayer and Spirit-filling; expands on the community economic life.
speaker: Narrator
summary: No one claimed private ownership — great grace was upon them; no one was needy.
interpretive_notes: Echoes Deut 15:4 ('there will be no poor among you'); early church as Jubilee embodied. Need-elimination is the stated goal.

literary_context: Appointment of the Seven (Acts 6:1–7); growth of the church creates an administrative mercy failure; the apostles create the diaconate to solve it.
category: A7 — Care for widows and orphans
speaker: Narrator
summary: Hellenist widows were being neglected — deacons appointed to ensure equitable distribution.
interpretive_notes: The church organizes structurally around the mercy need; the diaconate office is born from widow-care failure.

literary_context: Raising of Tabitha (Acts 9:36–43); Tabitha dies; Peter is summoned; widows mourn showing her clothing gifts.
category: A7 — Care for widows and orphans
speaker: Narrator
summary: Tabitha was full of good works and acts of charity — she was mourned by the widows she had clothed.
interpretive_notes: Mercy to widows is so central to her identity that her death triggers communal mourning; her works are her legacy.

literary_context: Cornelius vision (Acts 10:1–8); Roman centurion receives angelic confirmation; his prayers and alms are described as reaching God.
category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
speaker: Narrator / Angel
summary: Cornelius gave alms generously — his alms and prayers ascended as a memorial before God.
interpretive_notes: Almsgiving treated as prayer that rises to God; a Gentile's mercy shapes the course of redemptive history.

literary_context: Antioch church response to famine prophecy (Acts 11:27–30); first inter-church mercy collection; Barnabas and Saul carry it.
category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
speaker: Narrator
summary: The Antioch church sends relief to Judean brothers during famine — each giving as able.
interpretive_notes: First recorded inter-church financial mercy collection; 'according to ability' is proportional, not uniform.

literary_context: Paul's farewell to the Ephesian elders (Acts 20:17–38); Paul cites Jesus' words as the basis for his practice of giving.
category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
speaker: Paul
summary: Help the weak — it is more blessed to give than to receive, as Jesus said.
interpretive_notes: Only Pauline citation of a Jesus saying not in the Gospels; giving and helping the weak are grounded in Jesus' own words.

literary_context: Body of Christ and gifts (Rom 12:3–8); Paul lists spiritual gifts; generosity and mercy are included as charisms.
category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
speaker: Paul
summary: Giving with generosity and mercy with cheerfulness are listed as spiritual gifts.
interpretive_notes: Generosity is a charism, not merely a duty; mercy is named alongside prophecy and teaching as a spiritual gift.

literary_context: Christian ethics in community (Rom 12:9–21); a dense series of imperatives for community life.
category: A8 — Welcome the stranger / show hospitality
speaker: Paul
summary: Contribute to the needs of the saints and seek to show hospitality.
interpretive_notes: Two imperatives: meeting needs of the community and welcoming strangers; philoxenia literally means love of the stranger.

literary_context: Christian ethics in community (Rom 12:9–21); commands for communal social life.
category: A10 — Include the marginalized / seat the outcast
speaker: Paul
summary: Do not be haughty — associate with the lowly; do not think yourself wise.
interpretive_notes: Social equality as gospel ethic; haughtiness and status-seeking are explicitly forbidden.

literary_context: Christian ethics toward enemies (Rom 12:17–21); Paul quotes Proverbs to ground enemy-love in practical mercy.
speaker: Paul
summary: If your enemy is hungry, feed him; if thirsty, give drink — overcome evil with good.
interpretive_notes: Enemy-love expressed as literal food provision; grounds love of enemy in practical, bodily mercy.

literary_context: Christians and the law (Rom 13:8–10); Paul argues love of neighbor is the fulfillment of the entire law.
speaker: Paul
summary: All commandments are summed up in: love your neighbor as yourself.
interpretive_notes: Paul's version of the great commandment; parallel to Jesus in the Gospels; love is the fulfillment, not just a commandment.

literary_context: Paul's travel plans (Rom 15:22–33); Paul describes his upcoming journey and the Jerusalem collection as part of his apostolic mission.
category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
speaker: Paul
summary: Paul carries financial aid to the poor among the saints in Jerusalem — an apostolic mercy mission.
interpretive_notes: The collection is framed as a spiritual obligation; Gentile churches owe material support to the Jerusalem church.

literary_context: Lord's Supper abuse (1 Cor 11:17–34); some eat lavishly while the poor go hungry at the communal meal; Paul rebukes sharply.
category: A6 — Pursue economic equality / oppose exploitation
subtheme: Shaming the Poor at the Lord's Supper
speaker: Paul
summary: Some go hungry while others feast — you humiliate those who have nothing at the Lord's table.
interpretive_notes: Economic inequality at the Eucharist is treated as a desecration; shaming the poor is equated with despising the church of God.

literary_context: Love chapter (1 Cor 13); Paul places love as the indispensable foundation beneath all gifts and acts, including total self-giving.
category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
speaker: Paul
summary: If I give all I have to the poor but have not love, I gain nothing.
interpretive_notes: Generosity without love is empty; love is the motive that makes mercy meaningful.

literary_context: Practical instructions (1 Cor 16); Paul commands a systematic weekly collection for the Jerusalem saints.
category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
speaker: Paul
summary: Set aside a sum every week — systematic, pre-planned giving for the poor among the saints.
interpretive_notes: First explicit instruction for regular, systematic financial giving; proportional to prosperity.

literary_context: Appeal for the Jerusalem collection (2 Cor 8–9); Paul holds up the Macedonian churches as models of sacrificial giving.
speaker: Paul
summary: In severe poverty and great joy the Macedonians gave beyond their means — out of their own accord.
interpretive_notes: Poverty + joy + generosity as the model; they gave themselves first (v.5); grace (charis) is the word used for their giving.

literary_context: Appeal for the Jerusalem collection (2 Cor 8); Paul grounds generosity in the incarnation of Christ.
speaker: Paul
summary: Though rich, Christ became poor for our sake — his poverty is the model for our generosity.
interpretive_notes: The incarnation is the theological foundation of economic generosity; Christological grounding of wealth-sharing.

literary_context: Appeal for the Jerusalem collection (2 Cor 8); Paul frames the collection as seeking economic equality between churches.
category: A6 — Pursue economic equality / oppose exploitation
speaker: Paul
summary: Your abundance should supply their need — economic equality as the explicit goal.
interpretive_notes: Quotes Exod 16:18 (manna); abundance and lack should balance out; equality is the stated NT economic goal.

literary_context: Second appeal for the collection (2 Cor 9); Paul encourages voluntary, joyful giving.
category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
speaker: Paul
summary: Each one must give as decided in his heart — not reluctantly, for God loves a cheerful giver.
interpretive_notes: Giving from free decision, not compulsion; joyfulness is the posture; quotes Prov 22:8 LXX.

literary_context: Second appeal for the collection (2 Cor 9); Paul describes how generosity glorifies God and produces thanksgiving.
category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
speaker: Paul
summary: You are enriched in every way to be generous — generosity produces thanksgiving and glorifies God.
interpretive_notes: Generosity as doxology; the goal of giving is not the gift but the glory of God through it.

literary_context: Jerusalem Council agreement (Gal 2:1–10); Paul describes the accord between himself and the Jerusalem apostles; the one shared obligation is care for the poor.
category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
speaker: Paul
summary: The Jerusalem apostles asked only that we remember the poor — the very thing Paul was eager to do.
interpretive_notes: Apostolic consensus: care for the poor is the one unanimous obligation agreed at the highest-level church council.

literary_context: Freedom and the Spirit (Gal 5:1–26); Paul argues that freedom from the Mosaic law expresses itself as neighbor-love.
speaker: Paul
summary: The entire law is fulfilled in one word: love your neighbor as yourself.
interpretive_notes: Paul and Jesus agree on this summary; freedom from law is expressed as love, not license.

literary_context: Instruction on restoring the fallen and bearing burdens (Gal 6:1–5); mutual support in the community.
category: A11 — Bear one another's burdens / help the weak
speaker: Paul
gk_keywords: bastazō (bear); baros (burden); nomos Christou (law of Christ)
summary: Bear one another's burdens — this is the content of Christ's law.
interpretive_notes: Mutual burden-bearing is identified as the 'law of Christ'; supports the weak and fallen.

literary_context: Summary of community ethics (Gal 6:6–10); closing ethical instructions before the conclusion.
speaker: Paul
summary: As we have opportunity, do good to all — especially to the household of faith.
interpretive_notes: Universal scope of mercy; 'especially' implies wider obligation, not narrow exclusion to believers only.

literary_context: New life in Christ (Eph 4:17–32); practical ethical commands for transformed living; the purpose of work is reframed.
category: A3 — Give to the needy / almsgiving
speaker: Paul
summary: Work honestly — so that you may have something to share with anyone in need.
interpretive_notes: Labor's ultimate purpose is not self-sufficiency but redistribution to the needy; a radical re-framing of why one works.

literary_context: New life in Christ (Eph 4:17–32); mercy virtues as the character of transformed people.
speaker: Paul
summary: Be kind and compassionate — forgive one another as God in Christ forgave you.
interpretive_notes: Trinitarian mercy as the basis for horizontal mercy; divine forgiveness is the model and the motive.

literary_context: Christ's humility as the model (Phil 2:1–11); Paul grounds the call to unity and other-focus in the incarnation.
category: A12 — Servant leadership / serve rather than rule
speaker: Paul
summary: In humility count others more significant than yourself — look to the interests of others.
interpretive_notes: Counter-cultural social inversion grounded in Christ's self-emptying (v.6-8); other-focus as the core posture.

literary_context: New life in Christ (Col 3:1–17); Paul describes the character of the renewed person; mercy virtues are listed as identity clothing.
speaker: Paul
summary: Put on compassion, kindness, humility, meekness, and patience — as God's chosen people.
interpretive_notes: Mercy virtues described as clothing to be deliberately put on; identity of the new person is fundamentally merciful.

literary_context: Final exhortations (1 Thess 5:12–22); closing ethical commands; care for the vulnerable is listed alongside doctrinal faithfulness.
category: A11 — Bear one another's burdens / help the weak
speaker: Paul
summary: Encourage the fainthearted, help the weak — be patient with them all.
interpretive_notes: The weak are to be actively helped, not judged; patience is the posture toward all categories of vulnerability.

literary_context: Instructions about widows (1 Tim 5:1–16); Timothy is given detailed instructions for the church's care of widows.
category: A7 — Care for widows and orphans
speaker: Paul
summary: Honor widows who are truly widows — failing to provide for family is to deny the faith.
interpretive_notes: Church and family both bear responsibility for widows; failure to provide is equated with apostasy — the strongest possible language.

literary_context: Final instructions (1 Tim 6:17–19); Paul gives Timothy a direct command to pass on to wealthy church members.
speaker: Paul
gk_keywords: eumetadotos (generous/ready to share); koinōnikous (ready to share)
summary: Charge the rich to be generous and ready to share — storing treasure in heaven.
interpretive_notes: Direct pastoral instruction to wealthy believers; generosity is the antidote to wealth's spiritual dangers.

literary_context: Final instructions (Titus 3:12–15); Paul closes with a command to prioritize practical mercy.
category: A11 — Bear one another's burdens / help the weak
subtheme: Devote to Good Works / Urgent Needs
speaker: Paul
summary: Our people must learn to devote themselves to good works — to help cases of urgent need.
interpretive_notes: Good works specifically directed toward urgent needs; 'unfruitful' describes the failure to act mercifully.

literary_context: Letter to Philemon; Paul appeals for Onesimus, a runaway slave, to be welcomed back as a brother, not a slave.
category: A10 — Include the marginalized / seat the outcast
speaker: Paul
summary: Welcome Onesimus no longer as a slave but as a dear brother — receive him as you would receive me.
interpretive_notes: Social hierarchy overturned by gospel identity; 'in the Lord' redefines the relationship; Paul's personal appeal makes it a moral command.

literary_context: Closing exhortations (Heb 13:1–6); series of mercy commands following the great cloud of witnesses.
category: A8 — Welcome the stranger / show hospitality
speaker: Author
summary: Do not neglect to show hospitality to strangers — some have entertained angels unawares.
interpretive_notes: Philoxenia is a virtue command; the theological motivation is that the stranger may be a divine messenger.

literary_context: Closing exhortations (Heb 13:1–6); mercy commands for the community.
category: A9 — Visit prisoners / remember the suffering
speaker: Author
summary: Remember those in prison as if imprisoned with them — the mistreated as if in your own body.
interpretive_notes: Embodied solidarity: identification with the imprisoned and mistreated as if sharing their condition.

literary_context: Closing exhortations (Heb 13:15–16); generosity and sharing described as sacrificial worship.
speaker: Author
summary: Do not neglect to do good and to share — such sacrifices are pleasing to God.
interpretive_notes: Sharing framed as sacrificial worship; replaces the temple sacrifice system with mercy acts.

literary_context: True religion (Jas 1:22–27); James contrasts self-deception with genuine faith; mercy to orphans and widows is his definition of pure religion.
category: A7 — Care for widows and orphans
speaker: James
summary: Pure religion is to visit orphans and widows in their affliction.
interpretive_notes: James defines genuine religion not by doctrine or ritual but by mercy to the most vulnerable; widows and orphans are the OT's paradigmatic vulnerable group.

literary_context: Against favoritism (Jas 2:1–13); James rebukes the church for preferential treatment of the wealthy over the poor.
category: A10 — Include the marginalized / seat the outcast
speaker: James
summary: Show no partiality — you dishonor the poor; God chose the poor to be rich in faith.
interpretive_notes: Favoritism toward wealth is incompatible with faith in Christ; God's own choice of the poor inverts the congregation's social hierarchy.

literary_context: Against favoritism and for mercy (Jas 2:1–13); closing statement of the section on favoritism.
speaker: James
summary: Judgment is without mercy to the one who shows no mercy — mercy triumphs over judgment.
interpretive_notes: Mercy to others is the criterion by which one receives mercy; the most compact statement of mercy's eschatological stakes.

literary_context: Faith and works (Jas 2:14–26); James uses a poverty scenario — a hungry and naked brother — as the test case for living faith.
category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
subtheme: Faith Without Works — Food and Clothing
speaker: James
summary: Faith without feeding the hungry and clothing the naked is dead — a mercy scenario tests genuine faith.
interpretive_notes: James selects a poverty scenario — not a doctrinal scenario — as the litmus test for living faith; the test is mercy.

literary_context: Woe to the rich (Jas 5:1–6); prophetic denunciation addressed to wealthy oppressors; the most explicit economic justice passage in the Epistles.
category: A6 — Pursue economic equality / oppose exploitation
speaker: James
gk_keywords: misthoi (wages); apesterēmenos (kept back by fraud); kraugazō (cry out)
summary: Weep for the misery coming — you have defrauded the workers of their wages.
interpretive_notes: Prophetic denunciation of wage theft; wages crying out echoes Deut 24:14-15; the most direct NT economic justice passage.

literary_context: Living as God's people (1 Pet 3:8–12); community ethics for Christians living as aliens in hostile society.
speaker: Peter
summary: Have unity, sympathy, brotherly love, a tender heart, and a humble mind.
interpretive_notes: Mercy disposition as community ethic for believers under social pressure; tenderness is a command, not a suggestion.

literary_context: Living as stewards of grace (1 Pet 4:7–11); closing community ethics; love, hospitality, and service are primary.
category: A8 — Welcome the stranger / show hospitality
speaker: Peter
summary: Love earnestly, show hospitality without grumbling, serve one another — as stewards of God's grace.
interpretive_notes: Hospitality and service framed as stewardship of grace; love covers sin through practical mercy.

literary_context: Abiding in love (1 John 3:11–18); John argues that love must be concrete; uses a poverty scenario to test whether God's love indwells.
category: A2 — Feed the hungry / provide for bodily needs
speaker: John
summary: If you have goods and see a brother in need but close your heart — God's love does not abide in you.
interpretive_notes: Closing the heart against a needy brother is the test case for the absence of God's love; love is in deed, not word.

literary_context: God is love (1 John 4:7–21); John grounds horizontal love in God's nature and in the sending of Christ.
speaker: John
summary: Let us love one another — love is from God; whoever loves is born of God and knows God.
interpretive_notes: Love of others is evidence of regeneration and knowledge of God; its absence is evidence of not knowing God.

literary_context: Commendation of Gaius (3 John 1–8); John commends a believer for supporting travelling missionaries and strangers.
category: A8 — Welcome the stranger / show hospitality
speaker: John
summary: You are faithful in what you do for brothers and strangers — send them on in a manner worthy of God.
interpretive_notes: Hospitality to strangers and travelling gospel workers is active support, not merely shelter; 'worthy of God' sets the standard.

literary_context: Contend for the faith and show mercy (Jude 20–23); closing exhortation — mercy to the spiritually vulnerable is commanded alongside doctrinal faithfulness.
speaker: Jude
summary: Have mercy on those who doubt — save others by snatching them from fire.
interpretive_notes: Mercy as active rescue of the spiritually vulnerable; three categories of those in danger each require differentiated mercy.

literary_context: Letter to Thyatira (Rev 2:18–29); Christ commends the church; love and service are listed first among the evidences of faithful life.
category: A12 — Servant leadership / serve rather than rule
subtheme: Love and Service as Kingdom Credentials
speaker: Christ / Angel
summary: I know your love, faith, service, and endurance — your latter works exceed the first.
interpretive_notes: Love and service named first among kingdom credentials; increasing works of mercy are commended.

literary_context: Letter to Laodicea (Rev 3:14–22); Christ rebukes the church for self-sufficient wealth and calls them to recognize their true poverty.
speaker: Christ / Angel
summary: You say 'I am rich and need nothing' — but you are wretched, poor, blind, and naked.
interpretive_notes: Self-sufficient wealth produces spiritual blindness; the language of poverty, blindness, and nakedness echoes Jesus' mercy categories ironically.

literary_context: Luke 6:20-49 — Sermon on the Plain; the love-of-enemies section is the fuller Lukan parallel to Matt 5:43-44 and the more elaborated form of the command.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Love your enemies, do good to those who hate you, bless those who curse you, pray for abusers.
interpretive_notes: Luke's version elaborates the command with four specific actions; fuller than Matthew's version. The address 'you who hear' suggests selective reception.

literary_context: Luke 6 — Sermon on the Plain; the rationale section following the love-of-enemies command; contrasts disciple-love with ordinary reciprocal love.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Love your enemies and do good without expecting return — this is the mark of sons of the Most High.
interpretive_notes: The command to lend expecting nothing in return bridges A1 and A5 themes; divine impartiality is the grounding argument.

literary_context: 1 Thess 4:1-12 — ethical instructions; Paul affirms and reinforces the Thessalonians' practice of mutual love.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: You have been taught by God to love one another — continue to do so more and more.
interpretive_notes: The remarkable word theodidaktos ('God-taught') grounds the love command in divine instruction rather than apostolic authority.

literary_context: 1 Pet 1:13-25 — call to holiness; love is the natural outcome of purification through obedience to the truth.
speaker: Peter
summary: Love one another earnestly from a pure heart — purification by the truth enables genuine love.
interpretive_notes: ektenos implies sustained, strenuous love; this is not incidental but the culmination of spiritual formation described in vv.13-21.

literary_context: Heb 13:1-6 — closing ethical instructions; a rapid series of imperatives beginning with mutual love.
speaker: Author of Hebrews
summary: Let brotherly love continue — maintain the existing community of love.
interpretive_notes: The imperative menō ('keep on') assumes the community already practices love; the command is to sustain it rather than initiate it.

literary_context: 1 John 3:11-18 — love is the test of being children of God vs. children of the devil; Cain is the counter-example.
speaker: John
summary: Love one another — this is the original message from the beginning.
interpretive_notes: John roots the love command in the foundational apostolic proclamation, not a new ethical innovation; frames love as identity-constituting.

literary_context: 1 John 3:19-24 — assurance before God and the double commandment; faith and love are combined as a single command.
subtheme: Faith and Love as Double Command
speaker: John
summary: His commandment is to believe in Jesus Christ and to love one another — these two together constitute 'the commandment'.
interpretive_notes: The pairing of belief and love as a single entolē is theologically significant: orthodoxy and orthopraxy are inseparable.

literary_context: 2 John 1:4-6 — John emphasizes that the love command is ancient, not novel; contextualised against false teachers in the community.
speaker: John (Elder)
gk_keywords: agapaō; entolē; ap' archēs (from the beginning)
summary: Love one another — not a new commandment but the one from the beginning.
interpretive_notes: The insistence on 'not a new commandment' answers those who might regard the love command as a Johannine innovation.

literary_context: John 15:12-17 — the farewell discourse; Jesus summarizes the purpose of his commands as mutual love; the final statement of his friendship with disciples.
subtheme: Mutual Love — Purpose of All Commands
speaker: Jesus
summary: The purpose of all Jesus' commands is that you love one another.
interpretive_notes: This is the climactic statement of the farewell discourse: all other commands serve this goal; love is not one command among many but the telos of the whole.

literary_context: Rom 15:1-13 — climax of the strong/weak argument; mutual welcome is grounded in Christ's welcome of both groups.
category: A8 — Welcome the stranger / show hospitality
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Welcome one another just as Christ welcomed you — for the glory of God.
interpretive_notes: proslambanō used of God in Rom 14:3 and here; the welcome of the other person is grounded in and modeled on divine welcome.

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5:38-42); the fifth antithesis; non-resistance and generosity toward those who ask.
category: A5 — Lend without return / cancel debts
speaker: Jesus
summary: Do not refuse the one who wants to borrow from you.
interpretive_notes: The command is absolute — no conditions for creditworthiness; this verse bridges almsgiving (A3) and the lending/lending-without-return theme of A5.

literary_context: 1 Tim 5:3-16 — detailed instructions on widows in the church; qualifications for the widow-list, warnings about younger widows, and obligations of believing families.
category: A7 — Care for widows and orphans
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Enroll genuine widows aged 60+ on the support list; families must care for their own widows.
interpretive_notes: This passage shows that early church care for widows was institutionally organized; Paul's primary principle is that the church is a backstop, not a replacement for family care.

literary_context: Mark 12:38-40 — warning against the scribes; Jesus condemns exploitation of the vulnerable under cover of religious respectability.
category: A7 — Care for widows and orphans
speaker: Jesus
summary: Those who exploit widows while performing religious acts will receive greater condemnation.
interpretive_notes: This is a prohibitive command through the form of a warning; economic exploitation of vulnerable groups under religious cover is treated as especially grave.

literary_context: Matt 25:31-46 — the Sheep and Goats judgment parable; the criterion of judgment is how the nations treated the 'least of these my brothers'.
category: A8 — Welcome the stranger / show hospitality
speaker: Jesus
summary: Welcoming the stranger is identified with welcoming Christ; failure to do so is failure toward Christ.
interpretive_notes: This passage sets the eschatological stakes of hospitality; the stranger is identified with Jesus himself. Already appears under A2 for the hunger dimension; cross-listed here for the stranger/hospitality dimension.

literary_context: Matt 25:31-46 — Sheep and Goats; visiting the imprisoned is one of the six acts of mercy that identify treatment of Christ.
category: A9 — Visit prisoners / remember the suffering
speaker: Jesus
summary: Visiting the imprisoned is identified with visiting Christ; neglect is neglect of Christ himself.
interpretive_notes: The eschatological framing makes this one of the highest-stakes mercy commands; the criterion for final judgment.

literary_context: Heb 10:32-39 — appeal to remember their earlier endurance; the community's previous care for imprisoned believers is held up as a model.
category: A9 — Visit prisoners / remember the suffering
speaker: Author of Hebrews
summary: You showed compassion for those in prison — this is held up as exemplary mercy.
interpretive_notes: The past practice of prison-compassion is cited approvingly as evidence of genuine faith; forms the basis of Heb 13:3's direct imperative.

literary_context: Mark 9:33-37 — the disciples argue about greatness; Jesus reframes greatness in terms of servanthood and welcome of children.
category: A12 — Servant leadership / serve rather than rule
speaker: Jesus
summary: Whoever wants to be first must be last and servant of all.
interpretive_notes: Servant of 'all' (pas) is broader than servant of fellow disciples — encompasses unrestricted service; the sitting posture marks this as a formal, authoritative teaching.

literary_context: Luke 9:46-48 — the disciples argue about greatness; Jesus places a child in their midst as the model of kingdom greatness.
category: A12 — Servant leadership / serve rather than rule
speaker: Jesus
summary: The one who is least among you is the greatest — welcoming the child in Jesus' name is welcoming Jesus.
interpretive_notes: Luke frames greatness through receptivity to the lowly; the child is not a metaphor for the simple-hearted but a literal representative of the overlooked.

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 6); the immediate follow-on to the Lord's Prayer (6:9-13); Jesus unpacks the forgiveness petition.
speaker: Jesus
summary: God's forgiveness of you is contingent on your forgiveness of others.
interpretive_notes: One of Jesus' sharpest statements on the mutuality of forgiveness; the condition is not merit but posture — the unforgiving heart is incompatible with receiving divine mercy.

literary_context: Mark 11:20-25 — the withered fig tree and the saying on faith and prayer; the forgiveness condition is appended to the mountain-moving faith saying.
speaker: Jesus
summary: When you pray, forgive anyone you have anything against, so that your Father may forgive your trespasses.
interpretive_notes: Forgiveness is placed as a prerequisite to effective prayer; the command is unqualified — 'anything against anyone'.

literary_context: Luke 6:27-38 — Sermon on the Plain; the mercy and non-judgment commands follow the love-of-enemies section.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Be merciful as God is merciful; do not judge or condemn; forgive and you will be forgiven.
interpretive_notes: The Lukan parallel to Matt 5:48 ('be perfect'); mercy rather than perfection is the framing category. The list of prohibitions (judge/condemn) makes the mercy command concrete.

literary_context: Luke 17:1-4 — teaching on stumbling blocks, sin, and forgiveness; the forgiveness command is given a quantitative urgency (seven times in a day).
speaker: Jesus
summary: If a brother sins and repents — even seven times in a day — you must forgive him.
interpretive_notes: The command is conditional on repentance (unlike Matt 18:22 which implies unconditional); the combination of rebuke and forgiveness shows accountability and mercy are not opposites.

literary_context: Col 3:12-17 — the 'put on' passage; a list of relational virtues climaxing in love; forgiveness is grounded in the Lord's prior forgiveness.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Bear with one another and forgive each other as the Lord has forgiven you.
interpretive_notes: charizomai ('grace to each other') is the forgiveness word here — rooted in undeserved grace; the Lord's forgiveness is the explicit model and ground.

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5–7); following the Lord's Prayer and teaching on anxiety; transitions to the log-and-speck metaphor.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Do not judge, or you will be judged by the same measure — remove the log from your own eye before addressing the speck in your brother's.
interpretive_notes: One of the most frequently quoted NT commands. The prohibition is against hypocritical or self-righteous judgment, not moral discernment (cf. Matt 18:15-17). Luke's parallel adds "do not condemn."

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5–7); summarising statement before the narrow gate; Jesus says this sums up the Law and the Prophets.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Whatever you wish that others would do to you, do also to them — this is the Law and the Prophets.
interpretive_notes: The "Golden Rule." Jesus frames the positive formulation (do) rather than the negative (do not), elevating active love over passive avoidance. Hillel taught the negative form.

literary_context: Ephesians 4 opens the paraenetic (ethical) section; Paul transitions from theology (chs. 1-3) to conduct.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: axiōs peripateō (walk worthily); tapeinophrosynē (humility); praÿtēs (gentleness); makrothymia (patience); anechomai (bearing with)
summary: Walk worthy of the calling — with all humility, gentleness, patience, bearing with one another in love, eager to maintain the unity of the Spirit.
interpretive_notes: The central ethical imperative of Ephesians; all subsequent commands flow from this walking-worthy paradigm. The virtues listed are relational, not private.

literary_context: Ephesians 5:1-21; continuation of the ethical section; follows the forgiveness command (4:32) and introduces the walk-in-love imperative.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Be imitators of God, as beloved children, and walk in love — as Christ loved us and gave himself up as a fragrant offering and sacrifice.
interpretive_notes: The imitation-of-God imperative grounds all subsequent ethics in divine love rather than mere rule-keeping. Christ's self-sacrifice is the model.

literary_context: James 1:19-27; the hearing-doing section; immediately precedes the "doers of the word" command (1:22).
speaker: James (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: tachys eis to akousai (quick to hear); bradys eis to lalēsai (slow to speak); bradys eis orgēn (slow to anger)
summary: Let every person be quick to hear, slow to speak, slow to anger — for the anger of man does not produce the righteousness of God.
interpretive_notes: Three-part wisdom imperative for relational life. Human anger is contrasted with divine righteousness — anger fails to produce what God requires.

literary_context: James 4; follows the humility imperatives (4:7-10); speech as the arena where community loyalty is tested.
speaker: James (apostolic letter)
summary: Do not speak evil against one another — whoever speaks against a brother judges him; and whoever judges the law puts himself above the law.
interpretive_notes: Links slander to judging and to a posture of superiority over the law itself. Community-destroying speech violates the royal law of love (2:8).

literary_context: John the Baptist's preaching in the wilderness (Matt 3:1–12); the inaugural call preceding Jesus' ministry.
speaker: John the Baptist
summary: Repent — the kingdom of heaven is at hand.
interpretive_notes: Opening word of John's ministry; repentance as preparation for the kingdom.

literary_context: Beginning of Jesus' Galilean ministry (Matt 4:12–17); Jesus opens with the same word as John.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Repent — the kingdom of heaven is at hand. Jesus opens his ministry with the same call as John.
interpretive_notes: First public word of Jesus' ministry; continuity with John; repentance precedes all other discipleship.

literary_context: Beginning of Jesus' ministry (Mark 1:14–15); Mark's summary of Jesus' inaugural proclamation.
speaker: Jesus
summary: The time is fulfilled — repent and believe in the gospel.
interpretive_notes: Mark pairs repentance and faith as the twin response to the gospel; the fullest summary of the inaugural call.

literary_context: Repent or perish (Luke 13:1–5); Jesus responds to news of Galilean massacre and tower collapse; applies both to all.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Unless you repent, you will all likewise perish.
interpretive_notes: Universal application; repentance is not optional — it is the condition of life.

literary_context: Post-resurrection commission (Luke 24:44–49); Jesus summarizes the content of the disciples' mission.
subtheme: Repentance for Forgiveness — to All Nations
speaker: Jesus
summary: Repentance for forgiveness of sins is to be proclaimed to all nations.
interpretive_notes: Repentance is the core content of the universal mission; the risen Christ personally defines the message.

literary_context: Pentecost sermon (Acts 2:14–41); crowd asks 'What shall we do?'; Peter gives the definitive apostolic answer.
speaker: Peter
summary: Repent and be baptized — every one of you — for forgiveness and the gift of the Spirit.
interpretive_notes: The three-part apostolic call: repentance, baptism, Spirit. First proclamation after Pentecost.

literary_context: Solomon's portico sermon (Acts 3:11–26); Peter preaches after healing the lame man.
speaker: Peter
summary: Repent and turn back — that your sins may be blotted out.
interpretive_notes: Two verbs: repent (internal change) and turn back (directional change); together they describe full conversion.

literary_context: Areopagus sermon (Acts 17:22–31); Paul addresses Athenian philosophers; repentance is commanded universally to all people.
speaker: Paul
summary: God now commands all people everywhere to repent.
interpretive_notes: Universal scope; not invitation but command; addressed to philosophers outside Jewish tradition.

literary_context: Paul's relief at the Corinthians' response (2 Cor 7:5–13); Paul distinguishes genuine repentance from mere sorrow.
speaker: Paul
summary: Godly grief produces repentance leading to salvation — worldly grief produces death.
interpretive_notes: Qualitative distinction between genuine and superficial repentance; godly sorrow is the path to life.

literary_context: The day of the Lord (2 Pet 3:1–13); Peter addresses mockers who question the delay of Christ's return.
speaker: Peter
summary: God is patient — not wanting any to perish but all to come to repentance.
interpretive_notes: Divine patience is oriented toward universal repentance; the delay of judgment is an act of mercy toward the unrepentant.

literary_context: Letter to Ephesus (Rev 2:1–7); Christ rebukes the church for abandoning its first love; repentance is the commanded response.
speaker: Christ / Angel
summary: Remember, repent, and return to your first works — or the lampstand will be removed.
interpretive_notes: Repentance is required even of the most doctrinally faithful church; the call to repent appears in five of the seven letters.

literary_context: Discourse with Nicodemus (John 3:1–21); Jesus explains the new birth and the basis of salvation.
category: B2 — Believe / have faith in Christ
speaker: Jesus
gk_keywords: pisteuō (believe); monogenēs (only Son); aiōnios zōē (eternal life)
summary: Whoever believes in the Son will not perish but have eternal life.
interpretive_notes: The most widely cited verse in the NT; faith as the required human response to divine love.

literary_context: John the Baptist's final testimony (John 3:22–36); John affirms the Son's supremacy.
category: B2 — Believe / have faith in Christ
speaker: John the Baptist / Narrator
summary: Whoever believes has eternal life — whoever does not obey the Son shall not see life.
interpretive_notes: Faith and obedience are parallel; not-believing is framed as not obeying; wrath remains on the unbeliever.

literary_context: Bread of Life discourse (John 6:22–59); crowd asks what works they must do; Jesus names faith as the singular work.
category: B2 — Believe / have faith in Christ
subtheme: Faith as the Work of God
speaker: Jesus
summary: The work of God is this: believe in him whom he has sent.
interpretive_notes: Faith is framed paradoxically as 'the work' — it is the divinely-required human act.

literary_context: Lazarus raised (John 11:1–44); Jesus speaks to Martha before raising her brother; identity claim and faith demand.
category: B2 — Believe / have faith in Christ
speaker: Jesus
summary: I am the resurrection and the life — whoever believes in me will live even if he dies.
interpretive_notes: The most personal statement of resurrection faith; 'Do you believe this?' is a direct challenge.

literary_context: Farewell discourse (John 14:1–14); Jesus prepares disciples for his departure; trust is the commanded response to anxiety.
category: B2 — Believe / have faith in Christ
speaker: Jesus
summary: Do not be troubled — believe in God and in me.
interpretive_notes: Faith as the antidote to fear and anxiety; personal trust in Christ is the response commanded.

literary_context: Philippian jailer (Acts 16:25–34); earthquake; jailer asks 'What must I do to be saved?'; Paul and Silas answer.
category: B2 — Believe / have faith in Christ
speaker: Paul / Silas
summary: Believe in the Lord Jesus and you will be saved.
interpretive_notes: The simplest NT formulation of saving faith; household salvation included; response to the most urgent existential question.

literary_context: Righteousness through faith (Rom 3:21–26); Paul's most concentrated statement of justification theology.
category: B2 — Believe / have faith in Christ
speaker: Paul
summary: The righteousness of God comes through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe.
interpretive_notes: Foundational Pauline statement; faith is the instrument of justification; 'for all' removes ethnic limitation.

literary_context: Made alive in Christ (Eph 2:1–10); Paul describes salvation as entirely God's initiative and gift.
category: B2 — Believe / have faith in Christ
speaker: Paul
summary: You are saved by grace through faith — not by works; it is the gift of God.
interpretive_notes: The most concise statement of the grace-faith relationship; faith is the channel, not the cause, of salvation.

literary_context: Hall of faith (Heb 11); the chapter establishing faith as the defining characteristic of all OT heroes; verse 6 states the absolute necessity.
category: B2 — Believe / have faith in Christ
subtheme: Faith as Necessary to Please God
speaker: Author
summary: Without faith it is impossible to please God — whoever draws near must believe he exists and rewards seekers.
interpretive_notes: Faith is an absolute prerequisite; defines the relational posture required for access to God.

literary_context: Purpose statement (1 John 5:13–21); John closes the letter by naming its purpose — assurance of eternal life to believers.
category: B2 — Believe / have faith in Christ
subtheme: Belief and Assurance of Eternal Life
speaker: John
summary: I write so that you who believe may know you have eternal life.
interpretive_notes: Assurance of salvation is a gift to those who believe; certainty is the intended experience of faith.

literary_context: Mission discourse (Matt 10:24–42); Jesus prepares disciples for opposition; public confession has eternal consequences.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Everyone who acknowledges me before men I will acknowledge before my Father.
interpretive_notes: Public confession and denial have eschatological symmetry; confession is expected as a normal discipleship act.

literary_context: The word of faith (Rom 10:5–13); Paul describes the simple, accessible formula of salvation.
speaker: Paul
summary: Confess that Jesus is Lord and believe in his resurrection — you will be saved.
interpretive_notes: Mouth and heart together — public confession and internal faith are the twin acts of salvation.

literary_context: Christ's humiliation and exaltation (Phil 2:5–11); the great christological poem; the eschatological goal is universal confession.
speaker: Paul
summary: Every tongue will confess that Jesus Christ is Lord — to the glory of God the Father.
interpretive_notes: Eschatological universal confession; grounded in the kenosis of the incarnation; confession is the cosmos's proper response to Christ.

literary_context: Test the spirits (1 John 4:1–6); false prophets have gone out; confession of incarnation is the doctrinal test.
speaker: John
summary: Every spirit that confesses Jesus Christ come in the flesh is from God — denial marks the antichrist spirit.
interpretive_notes: Incarnational confession is the Christological test; a doctrinal boundary that distinguishes truth from falsehood.

literary_context: Great Commission (Matt 28:18–20); resurrection appearance; Jesus commissions the eleven.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the triune name.
interpretive_notes: Baptism is the rite of incorporation into discipleship; Trinitarian formula; universal scope.

literary_context: Pentecost sermon (Acts 2:14–41); also appears under B1 — here the focus is on baptism as commanded act.
speaker: Peter
summary: Be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of sins.
interpretive_notes: Baptism is the expected next step after repentance; linked to forgiveness and gift of Spirit.

literary_context: Paul's Damascus road testimony (Acts 22:1–21); Ananias's words to Paul after his encounter with Christ.
speaker: Ananias
summary: Rise and be baptized — wash away your sins, calling on his name.
interpretive_notes: Urgency of baptism ('why do you wait?'); washing metaphor; personal address to Paul himself.

literary_context: Dead to sin, alive in Christ (Rom 6:1–14); Paul grounds ethical transformation in baptismal union with Christ's death and resurrection.
speaker: Paul
summary: We are baptized into Christ's death and raised to walk in newness of life.
interpretive_notes: Baptism is theologically grounded in union with Christ; the ethical demand (walk in newness) follows from the baptismal identity.

literary_context: Noah's ark and baptism (1 Pet 3:18–22); Peter draws a typological connection between Noah's deliverance through water and baptism.
speaker: Peter
summary: Baptism saves you — as an appeal to God for a good conscience, through Christ's resurrection.
interpretive_notes: Baptism saves through conscience-appeal not physical washing; grounded in resurrection; carefully qualified statement.

literary_context: Mission discourse (Matt 10:16–23); Jesus prepares disciples for persecution; endurance is the response required.
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Jesus
summary: You will be hated for my name — but the one who endures to the end will be saved.
interpretive_notes: Endurance is the condition of salvation's ultimate fruition; persecution is assumed, not exceptional.

literary_context: Olivet Discourse (Matt 24:1–51); eschatological teaching; in context of false prophets and apostasy.
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Jesus
summary: Parallel to Matt 10:22 — the one who endures to the end will be saved.
interpretive_notes: Repeated in eschatological context; endurance in the face of cosmic upheaval.

literary_context: Peace with God (Rom 5:1–11); Paul describes the progressive fruit of tribulation in the justified life.
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Paul
summary: Suffering produces endurance, endurance character, character hope.
interpretive_notes: Endurance is not merely tolerated but a productive spiritual virtue; its telos is hope.

literary_context: Do not shrink back (Heb 10:32–39); author encourages believers facing persecution and loss of property.
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
subtheme: Need for Endurance to Receive Promise
speaker: Author
summary: You need endurance — so that after doing God's will you receive what is promised.
interpretive_notes: Endurance is positioned between present obedience and future promise; it is the connective tissue.

literary_context: Trials and wisdom (Jas 1:2–8); James opens by teaching that trials are occasions for developing mature faith.
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: James
summary: Count it all joy when you meet trials of various kinds — the testing of your faith produces steadfastness; let it have its full effect.
interpretive_notes: Steadfastness is the fruit of testing; James urges believers to let the process reach its full maturity.

literary_context: Letter to Smyrna (Rev 2:8–11); Christ addresses a persecuted church; tribulation is imminent; faithfulness unto death is commanded.
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: The risen Christ (via John)
summary: Be faithful unto death — I will give you the crown of life.
interpretive_notes: Faithfulness to the point of death as the ultimate expression of endurance; the reward is life itself.

literary_context: Farewell discourse (John 14:1–31); Jesus grounds commandment-keeping in love relationship.
category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
speaker: Jesus
summary: If you love me, you will keep my commandments.
interpretive_notes: Obedience flows from love, not obligation; the command is embedded in a relational frame.

literary_context: Farewell discourse (John 14); Jesus links commandment-keeping to the experience of divine love.
category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
speaker: Jesus
summary: Whoever keeps my commandments loves me — and will be loved by the Father.
interpretive_notes: Keeping commands is the evidence of loving Christ; divine self-disclosure is the reward.

literary_context: Vine and branches (John 15:1–17); Jesus grounds obedience in mutual indwelling; models it in his own relation to the Father.
category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
speaker: Jesus
summary: If you keep my commandments you will abide in my love — as I kept the Father's commandments.
interpretive_notes: Obedience is the condition of abiding; Jesus models it in his own relationship with the Father.

literary_context: Walking in the light (1 John 2:1–11); John identifies commandment-keeping as the test of genuine knowledge of God.
category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
subtheme: Commandment-Keeping as Evidence of Knowing God
speaker: John
summary: We know him if we keep his commandments — to claim knowledge without obedience is to lie.
interpretive_notes: Commandment-keeping is the epistemic criterion for knowing God; the claim without the practice is self-deception.

literary_context: The woman and the dragon (Rev 12); the devil persecutes the church; believers are defined by two marks.
category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
speaker: Narrator
summary: The dragon makes war on those who keep God's commandments and hold Jesus' testimony.
interpretive_notes: Commandment-keeping and testimony are the two defining marks of the persecuted church in Revelation; obedience is a combat posture.

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5:43–48); closing statement of the antitheses; love of enemy is grounded in divine perfection.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Be perfect as your heavenly Father is perfect.
interpretive_notes: Holiness grounded in divine character; 'perfect' (teleios) means complete, mature, whole — especially in love.

literary_context: Instructions in holiness (1 Thess 4:1–8); Paul names sanctification as God's explicit will.
speaker: Paul
summary: This is the will of God — your sanctification; abstain from sexual immorality.
interpretive_notes: Sanctification is explicitly named as God's will; holiness is not peripheral but God's stated purpose.

literary_context: Endurance as discipline (Heb 12:1–17); author calls believers to pursue the holiness that comes through discipline.
subtheme: Pursue Holiness — Without It None Will See God
speaker: Author
summary: Strive for the holiness without which no one will see the Lord.
interpretive_notes: Holiness is the condition of seeing God; it is to be actively pursued, not passively received.

literary_context: Living as exiles (1 Pet 1:13–21); Peter grounds holiness in God's own character and quotes Lev 11:44.
subtheme: Be Holy as God is Holy
speaker: Peter
summary: Be holy in all your conduct — as the one who called you is holy.
interpretive_notes: Direct quotation of Lev 19:2; holiness pervades all conduct; grounded in divine character.

literary_context: Letter of Jude; written to urge believers to resist false teachers who have crept in.
category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
speaker: Jude
gk_keywords: epagōnizomai (contend); hapax paradotheisē (once for all delivered)
summary: Contend for the faith that was once for all delivered to the saints.
interpretive_notes: Faith as a defined deposit to be defended; contending is active, not passive; false teachers have already entered.

literary_context: Paul's charge to Timothy (2 Tim 1:8–14); Paul urges Timothy to hold to the apostolic deposit.
category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
speaker: Paul
summary: Follow the pattern of sound words and guard the good deposit entrusted to you.
interpretive_notes: Sound doctrine is a deposit to be guarded; the Holy Spirit is the guardian's resource.

literary_context: Qualifications for elders (Titus 1:5–9); Paul lists requirements for church leadership.
category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
subtheme: Elder Must Hold to Sound Doctrine
speaker: Paul
summary: An elder must hold firm to trustworthy teaching — to instruct and to rebuke contradiction.
interpretive_notes: Sound doctrine is a leadership requirement; the elder must both teach it and defend it.

literary_context: Warning about false teachers (2 John 7–11); itinerant teachers who deny the incarnation are not to be welcomed.
category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
subtheme: Abide in the Teaching of Christ
speaker: John
summary: Whoever does not abide in Christ's teaching does not have God — do not receive false teachers.
interpretive_notes: The most exclusionary doctrinal command in John's letters; hospitality is restricted based on doctrinal faithfulness.

literary_context: Great Commission (Matt 28:18–20); post-resurrection; final command of Jesus in Matthew.
speaker: Jesus
gk_keywords: mathēteuō (make disciples); panta ta ethnē (all nations)
summary: Go, make disciples of all nations — baptizing and teaching them to observe all that I commanded.
interpretive_notes: The comprehensive mission: make disciples (not just converts); includes baptism and full ethical teaching — including mercy commands.

literary_context: Post-resurrection commission (Mark 16:14–18); Jesus addresses the eleven.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Go into all the world and proclaim the gospel to the whole creation.
interpretive_notes: Universal scope — all creation; the gospel proclamation is the primary act.

literary_context: Ascension (Acts 1:1–11); final words of Jesus before ascending; the Spirit empowers witness.
subtheme: Witness to the End of the Earth
speaker: Jesus
summary: You will be my witnesses — in Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, and to the end of the earth.
interpretive_notes: Concentric geographic mission; Spirit-empowered witness; provides the structural outline for the book of Acts.

literary_context: Israel and the gospel (Rom 10:14–17); Paul argues that hearing the gospel requires proclaimers; evangelism is the logical necessity.
speaker: Paul
summary: How shall they believe without hearing? How shall they hear without someone preaching?
interpretive_notes: The logic of mission: salvation requires faith, faith requires hearing, hearing requires preaching — preaching is indispensable.

literary_context: Paul's final charge to Timothy (2 Tim 4:1–8); Paul's last instructions before his death.
subtheme: Preach the Word in Season and Out
speaker: Paul
gk_keywords: kēryxon (preach); logos (word); eukairos / akairos (in/out of season)
summary: Preach the word — in season and out of season; reprove, rebuke, and exhort.
interpretive_notes: Pastoral urgency; preaching is non-negotiable regardless of circumstances; four specific acts: preach, reprove, rebuke, exhort.

literary_context: Living as God's people (1 Pet 3:13–17); Peter addresses believers under social pressure; readiness to explain faith is the command.
subtheme: Be Ready to Give a Defense
speaker: Peter
summary: Always be prepared to give a defense for the hope in you — with gentleness and respect.
