interpretive_notes: Personal witness in response to questions; gentleness and respect qualify the manner; hope is the visible quality that prompts questions.

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 6:5–8); triad of piety — almsgiving, prayer, fasting; Jesus teaches about authentic prayer.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Pray in secret to your Father — not for public show.
interpretive_notes: Private, unhypocritical prayer is commanded; the Father who sees in secret rewards.

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 6:9–13); the Lord's Prayer as a model for prayer.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Pray like this: Our Father... your kingdom come, your will be done. Daily bread. Forgive debts.
interpretive_notes: The Lord's Prayer contains both doctrinal petition and mercy content (forgiveness of debts); a comprehensive model.

literary_context: Parable of the persistent widow (Luke 18:1–8); Jesus introduces it with an explicit purpose statement.
subtheme: Pray Always and Not Lose Heart
speaker: Jesus
summary: Always pray and not lose heart — the widow's persistence is the model.
interpretive_notes: Perseverance in prayer is commanded; the parable immediately follows as illustration.

literary_context: Final exhortations (1 Thess 5:16–22); one of the briefest commands in the NT.
speaker: Paul
summary: Pray without ceasing.
interpretive_notes: The most compact prayer command in the NT; unceasing prayer as a life posture, not a single act.

literary_context: Armor of God (Eph 6:10–20); prayer closes and sustains the armor metaphor; it is the connecting thread of spiritual warfare.
subtheme: Pray at All Times in the Spirit
speaker: Paul
gk_keywords: proseuchomai (pray); Pneumati (in the Spirit); agrypneō (keep alert)
summary: Pray at all times in the Spirit — with all prayer and supplication, with perseverance for all the saints.
interpretive_notes: Prayer in the Spirit is the culminating piece of spiritual armor; intercession for others is included.

literary_context: Peace of God (Phil 4:4–9); Paul commands prayer as the antidote to anxiety.
speaker: Paul
summary: Do not be anxious — in everything let your requests be made known to God by prayer with thanksgiving.
interpretive_notes: Prayer as the structural replacement for anxiety; thanksgiving qualifies the posture of petition.

literary_context: Rev 2:12-17 — letter to Pergamum; the church is rebuked for tolerating those who hold to the teaching of Balaam and the Nicolaitans.
speaker: The risen Christ (via John)
summary: Repent now — otherwise Christ will come and war against those who hold to false teaching.
interpretive_notes: The ultimatum structure (repent or else) appears across the seven letters; the threat is corporate not merely individual, targeting the tolerated false teachers.

literary_context: Rev 3:1-6 — letter to Sardis; a largely dead church is called back to remembrance, keeping, and repentance.
speaker: The risen Christ (via John)
summary: Remember what you received, keep it, and repent — or Christ comes like a thief.
interpretive_notes: Three imperatives (remember, keep, repent) address the progressive spiritual decline; the thief-coming image echoes the parousia warnings in the Synoptics.

literary_context: Rev 3:14-22 — letter to Laodicea; the most severe rebuke of the seven letters; the lukewarm church is called to repentance with urgency.
speaker: The risen Christ (via John)
summary: Be zealous and repent — Christ disciplines those he loves.
interpretive_notes: The pairing of love with rebuke and discipline draws on Prov 3:12 / Heb 12:6; repentance is not optional but the loving response to divine correction.

literary_context: John 20:24-29 — the appearance to Thomas; Jesus directly commands belief in the resurrection.
category: B2 — Believe / have faith in Christ
speaker: Jesus (post-resurrection)
summary: Do not disbelieve but believe — Thomas is commanded to move from doubt to faith.
interpretive_notes: The direct command pisteuō addressed to an individual disciple is rare; Thomas's response ('My Lord and my God') is the christological climax of the Gospel.

literary_context: 1 John 3:19-24 — assurance and the double commandment; faith and love are presented as a single divine entolē.
category: B2 — Believe / have faith in Christ
speaker: John
summary: His commandment is to believe in the name of Jesus Christ — belief is not optional but commanded.
interpretive_notes: Unique in the NT in framing belief itself as a commandment (entolē); links faith and love as equally commanded.

literary_context: Mark 16:15-16 — the longer ending of Mark; the Great Commission with a baptism condition.
speaker: Jesus (post-resurrection)
summary: Whoever believes and is baptized will be saved.
interpretive_notes: The longer ending is textually contested but widely read; the pairing of belief and baptism mirrors Acts 2:38 and is the dominant early catechetical pattern.

literary_context: Acts 10:44-48 — the conversion of Cornelius's household; the Spirit falls before baptism, and Peter commands baptism as the proper response.
speaker: Peter (apostolic command)
summary: Peter commands the Gentile believers to be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ.
interpretive_notes: The passive reception of the Spirit before baptism (reversing the usual order) establishes that baptism follows faith, not produces it; yet it remains commanded.

literary_context: Heb 12:1-3 — the call to run with endurance; follows the 'hall of faith' in chapter 11; the cloud of witnesses grounds the appeal.
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Author of Hebrews
summary: Lay aside every weight and sin — run with endurance the race set before you, looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith.
interpretive_notes: The athletic metaphor grounds perseverance in communal encouragement (the cloud of witnesses); endurance is active, not passive resignation.

literary_context: 2 Tim 2:1-7 — three images of faithful endurance: soldier, athlete, farmer; Paul urges Timothy toward endurance through difficulty.
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: synkakopathō (share in suffering); stratiōtēs (soldier); Christos Iēsous
summary: Endure hardship together as a good soldier of Christ Jesus.
interpretive_notes: syn- prefix ('together') is significant — Paul calls Timothy to shared suffering, not isolated stoicism; the soldier image emphasizes disciplined focus.

literary_context: 2 Tim 4:1-5 — Paul's final charge to Timothy; the coming apostasy is described, and Timothy is called to faithfulness regardless.
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: nēphō (be sober); kakopathēson (endure suffering); euangelistēs (evangelist); plērophorēson (fulfill)
summary: Be sober, endure suffering, do the work of an evangelist, fulfill your ministry.
interpretive_notes: Four imperatives defining Timothy's calling as minister; 'do the work of an evangelist' also belongs to B9 (this verse cross-applies to multiple categories).

literary_context: 1 John 5:1-5 — born of God overcomes the world; love for God is defined as commandment-keeping.
category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
speaker: John
summary: Love for God is defined as keeping his commandments — and they are not a burden.
interpretive_notes: The denial of burden (bareiai) answers an implicit objection; love-as-obedience is not heavy compliance but relational fidelity.

literary_context: 1 John 3:19-24 — assurance before God; commandment-keeping is both the condition of answered prayer and the content of the double commandment.
category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
speaker: John
summary: Keep his commandments and do what pleases him — then whatever you ask you will receive.
interpretive_notes: The connection between commandment-keeping and answered prayer is direct; obedience creates the relational context in which petition is heard.

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 6:19-34); follows the command on treasures and the lamp of the body; the anxiety prohibition is grounded in the Father's provision and kingdom priority.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Do not be anxious about food, drink, or clothing — life is more than these things.
interpretive_notes: Jesus grounds the anti-anxiety command in creation theology and divine fatherhood; anxiety is the spiritual symptom of misaligned priorities ('seek first the kingdom', v.33). Moved here from D5 as a spiritual formation / holiness command rather than a worship-order instruction.

literary_context: Rom 12:1-2 — the hinge of Romans from theology to ethics; the foundational transformation command that governs chs.12-15.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: syschematizō (conform); metamorphoō (transform); anakainōsis (renewal); nous (mind); thelēma Theou
summary: Do not conform to this age — be transformed by the renewal of your mind to discern God's will.
interpretive_notes: The passive imperative metamorphousthe signals that transformation is divine work requiring human cooperation; the nous is the site of ethical discernment. Moved here from D5 as a sanctification command.

literary_context: Phil 4:4-9 — the closing ethical summary; the thought-life command follows the anxiety command and precedes the promise of the God of peace.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: alēthēs (true); semnos (honorable); dikaios (just); hagnos (pure); prosphilēs (lovely); logizomai (think/reckon)
summary: Fix your thinking on whatever is true, noble, just, pure, lovely, commendable, excellent, and praiseworthy.
interpretive_notes: The most explicit NT command about the content of thought; the list blends Greco-Roman virtue language with Jewish piety. Moved here from D5 as a sanctification/mind command.

literary_context: 2 Cor 6:14–7:1 — the call to separation from unbelief; a concluding exhortation grounded in the temple-indwelling promises of 6:16-18.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: katharizō (cleanse); molysmē (defilement); hagiōsynē (holiness); epitelesō (bring to completion)
summary: Cleanse yourselves from every defilement of body and spirit, completing holiness in the fear of God.
interpretive_notes: The command is self-directed (katharisōmen heautous — let us cleanse ourselves), not passive; holiness is both imputed (through Christ) and pursued (through self-cleansing).

literary_context: 1 John 3:1-3 — children of God and the hope of being like Christ; the eschatological hope motivates present purification.
speaker: John
summary: Everyone who hopes in Christ purifies themselves just as he is pure.
interpretive_notes: The indicative (purifies himself) functions as an imperative by definition — this is what hope in Christ entails; purity is both eschatological and present.

literary_context: 1 Tim 6:20-21 — the closing personal charge to Timothy; 'guard the deposit' is the final summation of the letter's doctrinal concerns.
category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: phylassō (guard); parathēkē (deposit/entrusted thing); kenophōnia (empty babble); pseudōnymos gnōsis (falsely called knowledge)
summary: Guard the deposit entrusted to you; avoid godless chatter and false knowledge.
interpretive_notes: parathēkē ('the deposit') is the technical term for something left in trust; Timothy is both guardian and steward of the apostolic teaching.

literary_context: Gal 1:6-9 — Paul's astonished rebuke at the defection from his gospel; the anathema is stated twice for emphasis.
category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: If anyone preaches a gospel different from what you received, let them be accursed.
interpretive_notes: The double repetition intensifies the warning; the criterion is the received apostolic gospel, not novelty. One of the strongest prohibitive commands in the NT regarding doctrinal deviation.

literary_context: Phil 1:27-30 — the central exhortation of the letter's first section; civic-virtue language (politeuesthe — live as citizens) applied to gospel community.
category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: politeuesthe (conduct yourselves as citizens); stēkō (stand firm); synathleō (strive together); pistis tou euangeliou
summary: Live worthily of the gospel, standing firm in one spirit and striving together for the faith.
interpretive_notes: synathleō ('striving side by side') is an athletic-competition metaphor; guarding the gospel is a communal, not merely individual, responsibility.

literary_context: John 20:19-23 — the first resurrection appearance to the disciples; the commission is grounded in the Father's sending of the Son.
subtheme: Sent as the Father Sent the Son
speaker: Jesus (post-resurrection)
summary: As the Father sent the Son, so the Son sends the disciples.
interpretive_notes: The Johannine mission-sending grounds the disciples' sending in the inner-Trinitarian sending; the scope is as broad as the Father's mission.

literary_context: Matt 9:35-38 — Jesus sees the crowds as harassed and helpless; the response commanded is not action but prayer for more workers.
speaker: Jesus
gk_keywords: deesthē (pray/beseech); kyrios tou therismou (Lord of the harvest); ekballō (send out)
summary: Pray to the Lord of the harvest to send out workers into his harvest.
interpretive_notes: Notably the response to the needy crowds is first prayer, not action; mission is sourced in prayer. The verb ekballō ('cast out') is the same word used for exorcisms — workers are thrust out with urgency.

literary_context: Luke 11:1-13 — Jesus teaches the disciples to pray; the Lord's Prayer is followed by the friend-at-midnight parable and the ASK/SEEK/KNOCK promise.
speaker: Jesus
gk_keywords: aiteō (ask); zēteō (seek); krouō (knock); lambанō (receive); euriskō (find); anoigō (open)
summary: Ask and you will receive, seek and you will find, knock and it will be opened to you.
interpretive_notes: The three imperatives are progressive; the present tense (keep asking, keep seeking, keep knocking) indicates persistence, not one-time petition.

literary_context: Matt 26:36-46 — Gethsemane; Jesus finds the disciples sleeping and issues the watch-and-pray command before his betrayal.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Watch and pray so that you do not fall into temptation — the spirit is willing but the flesh is weak.
interpretive_notes: The combination of watching and praying against temptation is the only explicit command in the Gethsemane narrative; prayer is the antidote to spiritual vulnerability.

literary_context: Col 4:2-6 — closing exhortations; prayer for the mission and for open doors for the gospel.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Devote yourselves to prayer — keeping alert in it with thanksgiving.
interpretive_notes: proskartereō ('devote/persist') is strong — the same word used for the early church's prayer in Acts 2:42; watchfulness in prayer guards against distraction and formalism.

literary_context: 1 Tim 2:1-7 — prayer for all people and rulers; the first priority of the gathered church is comprehensive intercessory prayer.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: deēsis (supplication); proseuchē (prayer); enteuxis (intercession); eucharistia (thanksgiving); hyper pantōn (for all)
summary: Urge that prayers of all kinds be made for all people — kings and those in authority.
interpretive_notes: The four-fold prayer vocabulary (supplication, prayer, intercession, thanksgiving) covers all modes; 'for all people' including rulers is the breadth the passage demands.

literary_context: Jas 1:2-8 — testing, wisdom, and prayer; wisdom for enduring trials is the specific object of the commanded petition.
speaker: James
audience: The twelve tribes in the diaspora
summary: If you lack wisdom, ask God who gives generously to all without finding fault.
interpretive_notes: The command is situationally specific (wisdom for trials) but the principle is universal; God's generous, non-reproaching giving is the basis of the command to ask.

literary_context: Jas 5:13-18 — prayer as the universal response to all states — suffering, joy, sickness, sin; the section climaxes with Elijah as a model.
speaker: James
audience: The twelve tribes in the diaspora
gk_keywords: kakopatheō (suffer); proseuchesthō (let him pray); euthumeō (be cheerful); psallō (sing praise)
summary: In suffering: pray. In cheer: sing. Prayer is the appropriate response to every circumstance.
interpretive_notes: The rhetorical questions (Is anyone...?) are implied commands; prayer is not a technique for crisis but the fundamental mode of every spiritual state.

literary_context: 1 John 5:13-15 — assurance of life and answered prayer; prayer according to God's will is the condition of confident petition.
subtheme: Confident Prayer According to God's Will
speaker: John
gk_keywords: parrēsia (confidence); aiteō (ask); kata to thelēma autou (according to his will); akouō (hear)
summary: If we ask anything according to his will, he hears us — and we have whatever we have asked.
interpretive_notes: The condition 'according to his will' is not a restriction but an alignment — prayer that conforms to divine purpose is the mode of confident petition.

literary_context: Closing exhortation of Ephesians; follows household codes (5:22–6:9).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: panoplia (full armor); diabolos (devil); pneumatika tēs ponērias (spiritual forces of evil)
summary: Put on the full armor of God — belt of truth, breastplate of righteousness, gospel shoes, shield of faith, helmet of salvation, sword of the Spirit — to stand against the devil's schemes.
interpretive_notes: [RESEARCHER TO COMPLETE]

literary_context: Closing exhortations of 1 Peter; follows instruction to elders and the young (5:1-7).
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: Be sober-minded and watchful — your adversary the devil prowls like a roaring lion. Resist him, firm in your faith.
interpretive_notes: [RESEARCHER TO COMPLETE]

literary_context: James 4 on friendship with the world vs. submission to God; part of a series of rapid imperatives (4:7-10).
speaker: James (apostolic letter)
summary: Submit to God and resist the devil — and he will flee from you.
interpretive_notes: [RESEARCHER TO COMPLETE]

literary_context: Olivet Discourse (Matt 24–25); Jesus responds to disciples' question about the end of the age.
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Jesus
summary: No one knows the day or hour of the Son of Man's coming — not even the angels or the Son, only the Father. Therefore, stay awake and ready; the Son of Man comes at an unexpected hour.
interpretive_notes: [RESEARCHER TO COMPLETE]

literary_context: Jesus teaching on discipleship and sin; follows rebuke of one casting out demons (9:38-42).
speaker: Jesus
summary: If your hand, foot, or eye causes you to sin, cut it off — it is better to enter life maimed than to go into Gehenna with two hands, where the worm does not die. Hyperbolic command for radical holiness.
interpretive_notes: [RESEARCHER TO COMPLETE]

literary_context: 1 Corinthians 6 on lawsuits and sexual immorality; follows rebuke of tolerance of sin within the church (5:1-13).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: adikoí (unrighteous); basileian theou ou klēronomēsousin (will not inherit the kingdom); planaō (be deceived)
summary: Do not be deceived — the unrighteous (including the sexually immoral, idolaters, thieves, the greedy, drunkards) will not inherit the kingdom of God. A broad repentance warning encompassing the full scope of moral failure.
interpretive_notes: [RESEARCHER TO COMPLETE]

literary_context: Paul's defense of his apostolic authority (chapters 10–13); responds to charges of weakness and inconsistency from opponents in Corinth.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: aichmalōtizontes (taking captive); logismos (thought/argument); hypakoē (obedience); hypsoumenon (exalting itself)
summary: We demolish arguments and every lofty opinion raised against the knowledge of God, and take every thought captive to obey Christ.
interpretive_notes: [RESEARCHER TO COMPLETE]

literary_context: Immediately after Peter's confession and Jesus' first passion prediction (16:13-23); Caesarea Philippi discourse.
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Jesus
summary: If anyone would come after me, let him deny himself, take up his cross, and follow me — whoever loses his life for my sake will find it.
interpretive_notes: The definitive discipleship demand; three imperatives in sequence. Cross-bearing is not metaphorical suffering but willingness to share Jesus' fate. Luke adds "daily."

literary_context: A Pharisee tests Jesus by asking which commandment is the greatest; Jesus responds with the Shema.
category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
speaker: Jesus
summary: Love the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, and mind — this is the great and first commandment.
interpretive_notes: The first and greatest commandment; paired with Matt 22:39 (love of neighbor) which is already in Dataset A. The Shema (Deut 6:4-5) is Jesus' starting point for all ethics.

literary_context: Upper Room Discourse (John 13-17); the vine and branches allegory; follows the new commandment (13:34) and precedes the world's hatred (15:18ff).
speaker: Jesus
summary: Abide in me, and I in you — as the branch cannot bear fruit by itself, neither can you apart from me. I am the vine; you are the branches.
interpretive_notes: The central Johannine imperative for the Christian life; "abide" (menō) appears 11 times in John 15. Fruitfulness is entirely dependent on relational union with Christ.

literary_context: Romans 6; Paul answers the objection "shall we sin so grace may abound?" — theological basis for sanctification after justification (ch. 5).
subtheme: Dead to Sin, Alive to God
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Consider yourselves dead to sin and alive to God in Christ Jesus. Do not let sin reign in your mortal body — present your members to God as instruments of righteousness.
interpretive_notes: Three imperatives: reckon, do not let reign, present. The first Pauline ethical command in Romans; flows directly from the indicative of union with Christ in baptism (6:3-4).

literary_context: Romans 12 opens the paraenetic section; "therefore" connects to the mercies of God expounded in chs. 1-11.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: paristēmi (present); thysian zōsan (living sacrifice); logikē latreia (reasonable/spiritual worship)
summary: Present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God — this is your spiritual worship.
interpretive_notes: Perhaps the single most important Pauline ethical imperative; the entire body — not just the soul — is the site of worship. "Living sacrifice" redefines sacrifice as embodied daily obedience.

literary_context: Galatians 5; Paul's freedom argument culminates: freedom is not for the flesh but for the Spirit. Follows "serve one another through love" (5:13-14).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: pneumati peripateite (walk by the Spirit); sarx (flesh); epithymia (desire)
summary: Walk by the Spirit, and you will not gratify the desires of the flesh.
interpretive_notes: The foundational pneumatological imperative: the alternative to legalism and license. Precedes the flesh/Spirit contrast list (5:19-23). Walk (peripateō) implies ongoing daily conduct.

literary_context: Ephesians 5:15-21; the wise-living block; precedes the household codes (5:22ff). The Spirit-filling is the basis for worship (5:19-20) and mutual submission (5:21).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: mē methyskesthe (do not get drunk); plērousthe en pneumati (be filled with the Spirit)
summary: Do not get drunk with wine — that is debauchery — but be filled with the Spirit.
interpretive_notes: Contrast command: drunkenness vs. Spirit-fullness. The present passive imperative (plērousthe) implies ongoing, continuous filling, not a one-time event.

literary_context: Philippians 2; immediately follows the Christ-hymn (2:5-11); Paul draws the ethical implication of Christ's self-emptying.
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: katergazomai (work out); sōtēria (salvation); phobos kai tromos (fear and trembling); energeō (work in)
summary: Work out your own salvation with fear and trembling — for it is God who works in you, both to will and to work for his good pleasure.
interpretive_notes: Holds together human responsibility and divine sovereignty in sanctification. Not about earning salvation but about actively living out what God has already begun.

literary_context: Colossians 3 opens the ethical section; builds on the "raised with Christ" motif from 2:12-13.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: zēteite ta anō (seek the things above); phroneite (set your minds); dexia tou theou (right hand of God)
summary: If you have been raised with Christ, seek the things that are above. Set your minds on things above, not on things on earth.
interpretive_notes: Two imperatives — seek and set your minds — grounded in the indicative of resurrection union with Christ. The ethical life flows from a reoriented worldview.

literary_context: James 1:19-27; the hearing-doing theme; follows "quick to hear, slow to speak" and the mirror analogy (1:23-25).
category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
speaker: James (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: poiētai logou (doers of the word); akroatai (hearers); paralogizomai (deceive)
summary: Be doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving yourselves.
interpretive_notes: Central to James' theology of active faith. Directly parallels Jesus' parable of the two builders (Matt 7:24-27). The word "deceiving" implies that hearing without doing creates a false sense of obedience.

literary_context: 1 Peter 5; closing exhortations to the community; follows instruction to young people (5:5) and precedes the devil warning (5:8-9).
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: Humble yourselves under the mighty hand of God so that he may exalt you at the proper time — casting all your anxieties on him, because he cares for you.
interpretive_notes: Two linked imperatives: humble and cast. The anxiety-casting is an act of trust that flows from humility. Ps 55:22 provides the OT antecedent. Among the most-quoted pastoral encouragement texts.

literary_context: 1 John 4; follows the "abide in him" section (3:24); introduces the criteria for testing: confession that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh.
category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
speaker: John (Elder)
summary: Do not believe every spirit, but test the spirits to see whether they are from God — for many false prophets have gone out into the world.
interpretive_notes: The primary NT command for spiritual discernment. The test is christological: does the spirit confess Jesus Christ come in the flesh (4:2-3)?

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5-7); immediately after the Beatitudes; Jesus defines the mission-identity of his followers.
speaker: Jesus
gk_keywords: halas (salt); phōs (light); lampō (shine); kalos erga (good works)
summary: You are the salt of the earth and the light of the world — let your light shine before others, so they may see your good works and glorify your Father.
interpretive_notes: Identity statements with imperative force — "let your light shine" is the explicit command. Salt that loses saltiness is useless; a city on a hill cannot be hidden.

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5-7); between the judging prohibition (7:1-5) and the Golden Rule (7:12).
speaker: Jesus
summary: Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and it will be opened — everyone who asks receives.
interpretive_notes: Three present imperatives indicating continuous action. The promise is unconditional in form; context (7:9-11) grounds it in God's fatherly generosity.

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5-7); conclusion section; the two ways — narrow gate vs. wide gate.
speaker: Jesus
gk_keywords: eiserchomai (enter); stenē pylē (narrow gate); thlibō (narrow/difficult); hodos (way/road)
summary: Enter through the narrow gate — for the gate is wide and the way is easy that leads to destruction, and many enter through it. The gate is narrow and the way is hard that leads to life.
interpretive_notes: The two-ways motif has deep OT roots (Deut 30:15-20; Ps 1). Jesus makes the choice urgent and the stakes absolute.

literary_context: Missionary Discourse (Matt 10); Jesus prepares the Twelve for persecution; between "you will be hated" (10:22) and "do not fear" (10:26).
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Jesus
summary: Do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul — rather fear him who can destroy both soul and body in Gehenna.
interpretive_notes: Reorients the hierarchy of fears; bodily death is not ultimate. Context is persecution, not abstract theology.

literary_context: Discourse on community life (Matt 18); disciples ask who is greatest in the kingdom; Jesus calls a child.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Unless you turn and become like children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven.
interpretive_notes: The "turning" is a form of repentance — away from status-seeking and toward dependence. Mark's parallel uses "receive the kingdom like a child."

literary_context: John 12; Jesus' final public discourse before the Farewell Discourse; the crowd asks about "the Son of Man."
category: B2 — Believe / have faith in Christ
speaker: Jesus
gk_keywords: peripateite (walk); phōs (light); pisteuete (believe); huioi phōtos (sons of light)
summary: Walk while you have the light, lest darkness overtake you — believe in the light, that you may become sons of light.
interpretive_notes: Two imperatives: walk and believe. The light is Christ himself (John 8:12); the urgency is temporal — the light will not always be publicly available.

literary_context: Romans 8; life in the Spirit; follows the flesh/Spirit contrast (8:1-11) and precedes adoption as sons (8:14ff).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: thanatoō (put to death); praxeis tou sōmatos (deeds of the body); pneuma (Spirit)
summary: If by the Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body, you will live — for we are debtors not to the flesh.
interpretive_notes: The Spirit-empowered mortification command. Not asceticism but Spirit-enabled killing of sinful patterns. Central to Reformed and Wesleyan sanctification theology.

literary_context: 1 Corinthians 10; the idol-food discussion; summary principle for all disputed matters.
category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God.
interpretive_notes: The most comprehensive single ethical principle in Paul — extends beyond specific commands to all of life. Key text for the soli Deo gloria tradition.

literary_context: 1 Corinthians 16; closing exhortations; four rapid imperatives before the love-command (16:14).
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: grēgoreite (be watchful); stēkete (stand firm); andrizomai (act courageously); krataioō (be strong)
summary: Be watchful, stand firm in the faith, act like men, be strong.
interpretive_notes: Four staccato imperatives forming a martial sequence. The verb andrizomai (be courageous/manly) echoes LXX military exhortations (Josh 1:6-9).

literary_context: Galatians 5; climax of the freedom argument; bridge between the allegory of Sarah/Hagar (ch. 4) and the ethical section (5:13ff).
category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: eleutheria (freedom); stēkete (stand firm); zygos douleias (yoke of slavery)
summary: For freedom Christ has set us free — stand firm, therefore, and do not submit again to a yoke of slavery.
interpretive_notes: The magna carta of Christian freedom. The "yoke of slavery" is the Judaizers' demand for circumcision and Torah-observance as conditions of salvation.

literary_context: Philippians 2; introduces the Christ-hymn (2:6-11); follows the call to unity and humility (2:1-4).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: phroneō (think/have this mind); en Christō Iēsou (in Christ Jesus)
summary: Have this mind among yourselves, which is yours in Christ Jesus.
interpretive_notes: Sets up the Christ-hymn as the paradigm for Christian community life. The "mind" (phronēsis) is not mere intellectual agreement but an orientation of will and disposition.

literary_context: Philippians 3; Paul's autobiographical renunciation of his credentials; pressing toward the goal.
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Forgetting what lies behind and straining forward to what lies ahead, I press on toward the goal for the prize of the upward call of God in Christ Jesus.
interpretive_notes: Athletic metaphor for Christian perseverance. Paul models what he teaches — the first person gives it authenticity. "Forgetting" is not amnesia but refusing to rest on past achievement.

literary_context: Colossians 3; the put-off/put-on section; between "put to death" (3:5) and "put on compassion" (3:12).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: apotithēmi (put off); orgē (anger); blasphēmia (slander); aischros logos (filthy speech); endyō (put on); neos anthrōpos (new self)
summary: Put off anger, wrath, malice, slander, and obscene talk. Put on the new self, which is being renewed in knowledge after the image of its creator.
interpretive_notes: The put-off/put-on metaphor (changing garments) structures Pauline ethics. The renewal is progressive and christological — toward the Creator's image.

literary_context: 2 Thessalonians 2; after the man of lawlessness discourse; Paul urges the church to stand firm amid eschatological confusion.
category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: stēkete (stand firm); krateite (hold to); paradoseis (traditions); logos (word); epistolē (letter)
summary: Stand firm and hold to the traditions that you were taught by us, whether by our spoken word or by our letter.
interpretive_notes: Key text for apostolic tradition — both oral and written. "Traditions" (paradoseis) refers to authoritative apostolic teaching, not human customs.

literary_context: 2 Timothy 2; Paul's charge to Timothy amid suffering; between the faithful-sayings and the false-teaching warnings.
category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: spoudazō (be diligent/do your best); dokimos (approved); orthotomounta (rightly dividing/handling); alētheia (truth)
summary: Do your best to present yourself to God as one approved, a worker who has no need to be ashamed, rightly handling the word of truth.
interpretive_notes: The classic pastoral charge for faithful Bible teaching. "Rightly handling" (orthotomounta) literally means "cutting straight" — accuracy in interpretation.

literary_context: 2 Timothy 2; Paul's instructions to Timothy; follows the vessels analogy (2:20-21).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: pheuge (flee); neōterikas epithymias (youthful passions); diōke (pursue); dikaiosynē (righteousness); pistis (faith); agapē (love); eirēnē (peace)
summary: Flee youthful passions and pursue righteousness, faith, love, and peace, along with those who call on the Lord from a pure heart.
interpretive_notes: Dual imperative: flee and pursue. The Christian life is both escape from and movement toward. The communal element ("along with those who call on the Lord") is striking.

literary_context: 1 Timothy 6; Paul's final charge to Timothy; follows the love-of-money warning (6:9-10) and precedes the doxology.
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: diōke (pursue); dikaiosynē (righteousness); agōnizou (fight); kalē agōna (good fight); epilabou (take hold); zōē aiōnios (eternal life)
summary: Pursue righteousness, godliness, faith, love, steadfastness, gentleness. Fight the good fight of the faith; take hold of the eternal life to which you were called.
interpretive_notes: Triple command structure: pursue, fight, take hold. The athletic/military metaphor combines with virtues. "Good fight" (kalos agōn) suggests nobility, not mere struggle.

literary_context: Hebrews 4; transition from the warning about the wilderness generation to the high-priestly Christology (chs. 5-10).
speaker: Anonymous author of Hebrews
gk_keywords: kratōmen (hold fast); homologia (confession); proserchomai (draw near); thronos charitos (throne of grace); eleos (mercy)
summary: Since we have a great high priest — Jesus the Son of God — let us hold fast our confession. Let us draw near with confidence to the throne of grace, that we may receive mercy and find grace.
interpretive_notes: Two exhortations: hold fast and draw near. The basis is Christological — Jesus is the sympathetic high priest. Access to the divine throne replaces the OT tabernacle system.

literary_context: 1 Peter 1; follows the praise section (1:3-12); opens the ethical exhortation section of the letter.
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: anazōsamenoi (gird up); dianoias (minds); nēphontes (sober-minded); elpisate (set your hope); apokalypsis (revelation)
summary: Prepare your minds for action, be sober-minded, and set your hope fully on the grace that will be brought to you at the revelation of Jesus Christ.
interpretive_notes: Three imperatives for the waiting community: prepare, be sober, hope. "Gird up the loins of your mind" echoes the Passover readiness (Exod 12:11).

literary_context: 1 Peter 2; the elect-exile identity (2:9-10) leads to the ethical imperative; opens the social-conduct section.
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: apechomai (abstain); sarkikos epithymia (fleshly desires); kalē anastrophē (honorable conduct); paroikos (sojourner); parepidēmos (exile)
summary: Abstain from the passions of the flesh — as sojourners and exiles — and keep your conduct among the Gentiles honorable, so that they may glorify God.
interpretive_notes: Exile identity grounds the ethical demand. Honorable conduct is missional — it leads outsiders to glorify God "on the day of visitation."

literary_context: 2 Peter 1; follows the divine-nature promises (1:3-4); the virtue ladder leads to the "make your calling sure" exhortation (1:10).
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: spoudē (diligence); aretē (virtue); gnōsis (knowledge); enkrateia (self-control); eusebeia (godliness); philadelphia (brotherly affection); agapē (love)
summary: Make every effort to supplement your faith with virtue, virtue with knowledge, knowledge with self-control, self-control with steadfastness, steadfastness with godliness, godliness with brotherly affection, and brotherly affection with love.
interpretive_notes: The "virtue ladder" — seven qualities building on faith and climaxing in love. The structure suggests progressive Christian maturity, not sequential stages.

literary_context: 1 John 2; between the "children, fathers, young men" addresses (2:12-14) and the antichrist warning (2:18ff).
speaker: John (Elder)
gk_keywords: mē agapate (do not love); kosmos (world); epithymia sarkos (desire of the flesh); epithymia ophthalmōn (desire of the eyes); alazoneia biou (pride of life)
summary: Do not love the world or the things in the world — the desire of the flesh, the desire of the eyes, and the pride of life are not from the Father but from the world.
interpretive_notes: Three categories of worldly desire mirror the temptation narratives (Gen 3:6; Matt 4:1-11). "World" (kosmos) here means the system of values opposed to God, not creation itself.

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5-7); conclusion section; follows the narrow gate (7:13-14) and precedes "by their fruits" (7:16-20).
category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
speaker: Jesus
gk_keywords: prosechō (beware); pseudoprophētai (false prophets); endyma (clothing); probaton (sheep); lykos (wolf)
summary: Beware of false prophets who come in sheep's clothing but inwardly are ravenous wolves.
interpretive_notes: The test is by fruit, not appearance. Echoes Deut 13 and Ezek 22:27. Jesus warns about deception within the community, not external threats.

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5-7); the final parable; two builders as the climax and application of the entire sermon.
category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
speaker: Jesus
gk_keywords: akouō (hear); poieō (do); phronimos (wise); petra (rock); mōros (foolish); ammos (sand)
summary: Everyone who hears these words and does them is like a wise man who built his house on the rock — the rain and floods came and it did not fall.
interpretive_notes: The parable functions as a summary command for the entire Sermon on the Mount: hearing without doing is catastrophic. The distinction is not knowledge but obedience.

literary_context: Missionary Discourse (Matt 10); Jesus sends out the Twelve; between the mission commission and persecution warnings.
speaker: Jesus
summary: I am sending you out as sheep among wolves — so be wise as serpents and innocent as doves.
interpretive_notes: Dual virtue: shrewdness and purity held together. The tension is deliberate — neither naivety nor cunning alone serves the mission.

literary_context: Ephesians 5:15-21; the wise-living block; precedes the Spirit-filling command (5:18) and household codes.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: peripateō (walk); akribōs (carefully); sophos (wise); exagorazō (redeem); kairos (time/opportunity); syniēmi (understand); thelēma (will)
summary: Look carefully how you walk — not as unwise but as wise, making the best use of the time, because the days are evil. Understand what the will of the Lord is.
interpretive_notes: Three imperatives: walk carefully, redeem the time, understand God's will. The ethical urgency is eschatological — "the days are evil."

literary_context: Colossians 2; bridge between the theological exposition and the ethical section; warns against the "Colossian heresy."
category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: peripateite (walk); blepete (see to it/beware); sylagōgeō (take captive); philosophia (philosophy); paradosis (tradition); stoicheia (elemental spirits)
summary: As you received Christ Jesus the Lord, walk in him, rooted and built up. See to it that no one takes you captive by philosophy and empty deceit according to human tradition.
interpretive_notes: Two commands: walk in Christ and beware of false philosophy. The Colossian "heresy" combined Jewish ritual with Hellenistic speculation.

literary_context: Colossians 3:12-17; the put-on section; summary statement before the household codes (3:18ff).
category: B6 — Keep commandments / obey God's moral law
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: pan ho ti (whatever); logos (word); ergon (deed); onoma kyriou Iēsou (name of the Lord Jesus); eucharistia (thanksgiving)
summary: Whatever you do, in word or deed, do everything in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through him.
interpretive_notes: The Colossians parallel to 1 Cor 10:31. Covers the totality of life — speech and action — under Christ's lordship.

literary_context: Hebrews 3; the wilderness-generation warning; between the "house of God" Christology (3:1-6) and the rest-of-God promise (ch. 4).
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Anonymous author of Hebrews
gk_keywords: blepete (take care); apistos kardia (unbelieving heart); aphistēmi (fall away); parakaleite (exhort/encourage); sēmeron (today); sklērynō (harden)
summary: Take care, brothers, lest there be in any of you an evil, unbelieving heart that falls away from the living God. Exhort one another every day, as long as it is called "today," that none of you may be hardened by the deceitfulness of sin.
interpretive_notes: Both individual vigilance and communal exhortation are commanded. The "today" urgency draws on Ps 95. Sin's deceit hardens gradually.

literary_context: Hebrews 5:11-6:3; the maturity exhortation; between the Melchizedek preview and the warning against apostasy (6:4-8).
speaker: Anonymous author of Hebrews
gk_keywords: pherōmetha (let us press on); teleiotēs (maturity/perfection); themelios (foundation)
summary: Let us leave the elementary doctrine of Christ and go on to maturity — not laying again a foundation of repentance, faith, baptisms, and other basics.
interpretive_notes: Hortatory subjunctive with imperative force. Lists six "elementary" doctrines that should not need constant re-laying. Spiritual stagnation is dangerous.

literary_context: Hebrews 12; conclusion of the "unshakeable kingdom" contrast between Sinai and Zion (12:18-27).
speaker: Anonymous author of Hebrews
gk_keywords: latreuō (serve/worship); euarestōs (acceptably); eulabeia (reverence); deos (awe); pyr katanaliskon (consuming fire)
summary: Let us offer to God acceptable worship, with reverence and awe — for our God is a consuming fire.
interpretive_notes: The consuming-fire image (Deut 4:24) grounds worship in the holiness of God. Grace does not eliminate reverence.

literary_context: Hebrews 13; closing exhortations; paired with 13:17 (obey your leaders) as bookends for the section.
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Anonymous author of Hebrews
gk_keywords: mnēmoneuete (remember); hēgoumenoi (leaders); ekbasis anastrophēs (outcome of way of life); mimeisthai (imitate); pistis (faith)
summary: Remember your leaders who spoke the word of God to you — consider the outcome of their way of life, and imitate their faith.
interpretive_notes: Two commands: remember and imitate. The leaders' faith — visible in the outcome of their lives — is the model to follow.

literary_context: James 3; the tongue section; follows the faith-and-works argument (ch. 2) and opens the teaching on speech.
category: B8 — Guard sound doctrine / contend for the faith
speaker: James (apostolic letter)
summary: Not many of you should become teachers, my brothers — for you know that we who teach will be judged with greater strictness.
interpretive_notes: A warning-imperative that restricts proliferation of teachers. Greater responsibility means greater accountability. Sets up the entire tongue discourse.

literary_context: 2 Peter 1; follows the virtue ladder (1:5-7); the practical result of growing in godliness.
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: spoudazō (be diligent); bebaian (sure/firm); klēsis (calling); eklogē (election); ptaiō (stumble)
summary: Be all the more diligent to confirm your calling and election — for if you practice these qualities you will never stumble.
interpretive_notes: Not about earning election but about confirming it through visible growth. "Never stumble" is the promised result of diligent virtue development.

literary_context: 2 Peter 3; closing exhortation; follows the day-of-the-Lord passage and Paul reference (3:14-16).
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: phylassō (be on guard); phero (carried away); athesmōn (lawless); planē (error); auxanō (grow); charis (grace); gnōsis (knowledge)
summary: Be on your guard lest you be carried away with the error of lawless people and lose your own stability. But grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ.
interpretive_notes: Dual closing imperatives: guard (defensive) and grow (positive). Knowledge of Christ is the antidote to doctrinal instability.

literary_context: 1 Corinthians 15; closing verse of the resurrection chapter; the ethical conclusion of the resurrection argument.
category: B5 — Persevere / endure to the end
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: hedraios (steadfast); ametakinētos (immovable); perisseuō (abound); ergon kyriou (work of the Lord); kopos (labor); kenos (vain)
summary: Be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord — knowing that in the Lord your labor is not in vain.
interpretive_notes: The practical payoff of the resurrection argument — if Christ is raised, our work matters. Three imperatives: be steadfast, be immovable, abound.

literary_context: 2 Corinthians 13; closing valediction; after Paul's defense of apostolic authority.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: chairete (rejoice); katartizō (be restored); parakaleō (be encouraged); auto phroneō (agree); eirēneuō (live in peace)
summary: Rejoice, aim for restoration, comfort one another, agree with one another, live in peace — and the God of love and peace will be with you.
interpretive_notes: Five rapid imperatives summarizing the community life Paul has been urging throughout 2 Corinthians. Reconciliation is the overarching theme.

literary_context: 1 Timothy 4; Paul's charge to Timothy regarding his conduct and ministry; follows warning about false teachers.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: kataphroneō (despise); neotēs (youth); typos (example/pattern); logos (speech); anastrophē (conduct); agapē (love); pistis (faith); hagneia (purity)
summary: Let no one despise you for your youth — set the believers an example in speech, in conduct, in love, in faith, in purity.
interpretive_notes: Age is overcome by exemplary character. Five domains of example-setting cover the whole of public and private life.

literary_context: Titus 2; instructions to various groups; between older women (2:3-5) and slaves (2:9-10).
speaker: Paul (via Titus)
gk_keywords: sōphroneō (be self-controlled); typos kalōn ergōn (model of good works); aphthorian (integrity); semnotēs (dignity)
summary: Urge the younger men to be self-controlled, showing yourself a model of good works — with integrity and dignity.
interpretive_notes: Self-control (sōphrosynē) is the single virtue demanded of young men in Titus. The emphasis is on exemplary conduct that silences critics.

literary_context: Jude; closing positive exhortation after the extended warning against false teachers (vv. 3-19).
speaker: Jude
gk_keywords: epoikodomeō (build up); hagiōtatē pistis (most holy faith); proseuchomai (pray); pneuma hagion (Holy Spirit); tēreō (keep); agapē (love); eleos (mercy)
summary: Build yourselves up in your most holy faith; pray in the Holy Spirit; keep yourselves in the love of God, waiting for the mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ.
interpretive_notes: Three-part self-care imperative: build (faith), pray (Spirit), keep (love of God). The only positive commands in Jude amid a letter dominated by warnings.

literary_context: Household code section (Eph 5:21-6:9); follows commands to wives/husbands and precedes command to masters; part of the 'submit to one another' framework.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Enslaved persons told to obey masters as if obeying Christ, with good will.
interpretive_notes: Embedded in a reciprocal household-code structure; Paul frames obedience theologically, not as endorsement of slavery per se. Modern interpreters debate whether this legitimises or subverts the institution.
q2_notes: No creation grounding; addresses a specific social institution that no longer exists. IC fails: consistent application would require accepting the legitimacy of slavery. Often reapplied to employer–employee relationships — a Purpose–Form transfer that is applied here but denied for analogous commands.

literary_context: Immediately follows the command to slaves (Eph 6:5-8); completes the reciprocal structure of the household code.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Masters told to treat slaves the same way (with good will), stop threatening, remembering God shows no partiality.
interpretive_notes: One of the few NT commands directed at the more powerful party in the household code; often overlooked in interpretive history.
q2_notes: Same institutional context as Eph 6:5-8. The master's command is less cited than the slaves' command, suggesting selective emphasis even within the same passage.

literary_context: Household code section in Colossians (3:18-4:1); parallel to Eph 6:5-9.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Enslaved persons told to obey masters wholeheartedly, working as for Christ.
interpretive_notes: Parallel to Ephesians passage; adds eschatological sanction (inheritance/judgment).
q2_notes: See Eph 6:5-8. The same Purpose–Form logic (faithful work as service to Christ) that is applied here to justify recontextualisation is denied for other social-code commands.

literary_context: Household code section (Col 3:18-4:1); completes the slaves/masters pair.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Masters commanded to treat enslaved persons justly and fairly.
interpretive_notes: Parallel to Eph 6:9; uses stronger justice language (to dikaion, isotēs — justice, equality).
q2_notes: The master-command is largely ignored even where the slave-command is reapplied.

literary_context: Pastoral letter instruction; part of Paul's social-order teaching in 1 Tim 5-6.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Enslaved persons told to honor masters so that God's name is not dishonored; especially to serve believing masters well.
interpretive_notes: Adds a missiological/reputational rationale: poor conduct by Christian slaves would discredit the gospel.
q2_notes: Paul grounds this in witness and the honor of God's name — not in social order. The command is structurally identical to 1 Tim 2:11-12 but is treated oppositely.

literary_context: Titus 2 household-code summary covering older men, older women, younger women, younger men, and slaves.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Slaves told to be obedient, honest, not argumentative, in order to adorn gospel teaching.
interpretive_notes: Evangelistic framing: slave conduct as testimony to the gospel. One of four Pastoral Epistle household code commands.
q2_notes: Grounded in witness ("to adorn the doctrine of God") — same grounding used to argue for women's submission in Titus 2:4-5.

literary_context: 1 Peter 2:11-3:12 household code section; follows call to live as sojourners and precedes wives/husbands code.
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: Servants commanded to submit even to harsh masters, following Christ's example of unjust suffering.
interpretive_notes: Uses Christ's passion as the paradigm for servant endurance; theologically loaded but also used historically to counsel patient suffering of injustice.
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: The specific form here is NOT separable from the purpose (C2=N): the command explicitly uses Christ's suffering under unjust authority as the model. If the form is culturally relative, so is the Christological argument. If the Christological argument is binding, so is the specific submission to unjust masters.

literary_context: Eph 5:21-6:9 household code; flows from 5:21 ('submitting to one another out of reverence for Christ'); addressed first in the wife/husband pair.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Wives commanded to submit to husbands as the church submits to Christ.
interpretive_notes: Most debated household-code command. Whether 'head' (kephale) means 'authority over' or 'source' is a major exegetical dispute. The command follows mutual submission in v.21.
q2_notes: C1=Y: grounded in Christ/church analogy (v.23). C4=N: consistent application requires wives to submit "in everything" (v.24) — a qualification most complementarians immediately moderate. The same Eph 5 passage commands husbands to love as Christ loved (v.25) with equal force; the wife-command receives disproportionate institutional emphasis.

literary_context: Shorter parallel to Ephesians household code; part of Col 3:18-4:1.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Brief command for wives to submit to husbands 'as is fitting in the Lord'.
interpretive_notes: The qualifier 'as is fitting in the Lord' suggests limits on the submission.
q2_notes: Notably does NOT appeal to creation order — only "as is fitting in the Lord." The weaker grounding of this parallel suggests the submission command is not uniformly grounded across the NT.

literary_context: 1 Peter 2:11-3:12 household code; immediately follows servant command (2:18); wives addressed before husbands (3:7).
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: Wives told to submit so that unbelieving husbands may be won to faith by behavior rather than words.
interpretive_notes: Provides evangelistic motivation; the submission is instrumentalised toward the husband's conversion.
q2_notes: Explicitly evangelistic purpose (C2=Y: purpose is winning the husband). The same Purpose–Form logic used here — that the form serves a purpose that could be achieved differently — is not applied to the submission command itself.

literary_context: 1 Cor 7 — Paul's extended response to questions about marriage and celibacy; notably mutual in its structure.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Both spouses commanded to fulfill conjugal duty; neither has sole authority over their own body.
interpretive_notes: Notably mutual: the wife's authority over the husband's body is stated as clearly as vice versa — unusual in ancient household codes.
q2_notes: This is a mutuality command — neither spouse withholds from the other. Universally applied and internally consistent.

literary_context: 1 Cor 7 — Paul distinguishes his own instruction from direct dominical command (cf. Matt 5:32; 19:9).
speaker: Paul citing dominical tradition
summary: Wives told not to divorce husbands; if separated, remain unmarried or be reconciled. Husbands must not divorce wives.
interpretive_notes: Paul attributes this specifically to 'the Lord' — echoing the dominical sayings on divorce. Limited exception clauses appear in Matthew's version.
q2_notes: Universally applied with contextual qualification for abuse/abandonment.

literary_context: Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5:17-48); one of the six 'You have heard... but I say' antitheses; follows prohibition on lust.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Divorcing a wife (except for sexual immorality) causes adultery; marrying a divorced woman is adultery.
interpretive_notes: Exception clause (porneia) unique to Matthew; debated whether it refers to pre-marital unchastity or ongoing adultery.
q2_notes: Grounded in creation (Matt 19:8 appeals to "from the beginning"). IC=P: consistently applied, but the exception clause is interpreted variously; some traditions add additional exceptions beyond porneia.

literary_context: Matt 19:3-12 — Pharisees test Jesus on divorce; Jesus appeals to creation order (Gen 1:27; 2:24).
speaker: Jesus
summary: Divorce and remarriage constitutes adultery, except in case of sexual immorality.
interpretive_notes: Parallel to Matt 5:32 but in a different context (controversy dialogue vs. sermon).
q2_notes: See Matt 5:32.

literary_context: Eph 5:21-33 household code; the husband command is longer and more demanding than the wife command, modeled on Christ's self-giving.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Husbands commanded to love wives with the same self-sacrificial love Christ showed the church.
interpretive_notes: The christological model (self-giving, sanctifying, presenting in glory) sets an extraordinarily high bar for husbands.
q2_notes: Universally applied and internally consistent. However, institutional emphasis is markedly less than the wife-submission command in the same passage (Eph 5:22-24), despite identical force.

literary_context: Household code in Col 3:18-4:1; brief parallel to Eph 5:25.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Husbands told to love wives and not be bitter/harsh toward them.
interpretive_notes: The prohibition on harshness (pikrainō) is practically significant; love is not merely emotional but behavioral.
q2_notes: Broadly applied. The prohibition of harshness is underemphasized relative to the love command.

literary_context: 1 Pet 3 household code; follows the extensive wife command (3:1-6); notable for the spiritual-equality clause.
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
summary: Husbands to live with wives with understanding, honoring them as co-heirs of grace, or prayers will be hindered.
interpretive_notes: The 'weaker vessel' phrase has been variously interpreted (physical, social, or situational); the co-heir clause asserts spiritual equality. The prayer-hindrance warning is unique.
q2_notes: "Weaker vessel" is interpreted variously — physical weakness, social vulnerability, or ontological distinction. The co-heir clause ("heirs together of the grace of life") is frequently underemphasized relative to the weakness clause.

literary_context: Eph 6 household code; follows wives/husbands section; child command precedes parent command.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Children told to obey parents; Paul cites the 5th commandment with its promise.
interpretive_notes: One of the few NT commands that explicitly cites and reaffirms OT law; the promise (long life in the land) is taken eschatologically by most interpreters.
q2_notes: Grounded in the Decalogue (C1=Y); universally applied.

literary_context: Household code in Col 3:18-4:1; parallel to Eph 6:1-3.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Children commanded to obey parents in everything as pleasing to the Lord.
interpretive_notes: Simpler than Ephesians parallel; 'in everything' is broader but qualified by the 'in the Lord' framing of Eph 6:1.
q2_notes: "In everything" is qualified implicitly by Christian obedience (cf. Acts 5:29).

literary_context: Eph 6 household code; the parent-side counterpart to the children's obedience command.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Fathers told not to provoke children to anger but to raise them in godly discipline and instruction.
interpretive_notes: Parorgizō (provoke to anger) — rare NT word; the positive command is to formation, not merely rule-enforcement.
q2_notes: Universally applied; but note that the prohibition of exasperation often receives less emphasis than child obedience.

literary_context: Col 3:18-4:1 household code; parallel to Eph 6:4.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Fathers told not to provoke/irritate children lest they become discouraged.
interpretive_notes: Uses erethizō (irritate/stir up) vs. parorgizō in Ephesians; the consequence here is discouragement rather than anger.

literary_context: Matt 15:1-9 — Jesus rebukes Pharisees for using Corban tradition to dishonor parents; cites the 5th commandment.
speaker: Jesus (citing OT law approvingly)
summary: Jesus reaffirms the command to honor parents and condemns religious loopholes that undermine it.
interpretive_notes: Significant: Jesus here defends the OT command against religious traditions that effectively nullify it.
q2_notes: C4=N: The death-penalty clause is universally treated as the OT civil law, not binding on the church — an implicit application of Purpose–Form logic and historical-context reasoning that is denied for other Mosaic-law citations in the NT.

literary_context: 1 Cor 11:2-16 — discussion of head coverings in corporate worship; Paul appeals to creation order, nature, and Corinthian practice.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Women praying or prophesying in worship must cover their heads or else should shave their heads (as a disgrace).
interpretive_notes: Debated: whether 'head covering' is cultural (Roman/Jewish custom) or universally normative. Paul grounds it both in creation order AND custom, creating interpretive complexity.
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: Paul appeals to "glory" and "angels" as grounding (C1=P). C4=N: the same chapter assumes women CAN pray and prophesy (11:5) — if 14:34-35 (women silent) is universal, this contradicts 11:5. Most evangelicals treat head coverings as cultural while treating women-not-teaching as binding — but both are in Pauline epistles, both address gender in corporate worship.

literary_context: 1 Cor 11:2-16 head-covering discussion; Paul appeals to 'nature' as corroborating evidence.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Nature teaches that long hair is shameful for men but glorious for women.
interpretive_notes: The appeal to 'nature' here is culturally contingent for many interpreters; hair length norms vary across cultures and eras.
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: Paul appeals to "nature itself" (C1=P — as strong a grounding as creation order). Yet virtually no evangelical church enforces a rule against men having long hair. The same "nature" argument is not treated as binding here but analogous arguments elsewhere are. The criterion is applied selectively.

literary_context: 1 Tim 2:1-15 — instructions on prayer and conduct in worship; Paul gives directions to both men (v.8) and women (vv.9-15).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: proseuchomai; epairō; hosias cheiras; orge; dialogismos
summary: Men instructed to pray with lifted holy hands, free from anger and dispute.
interpretive_notes: The physical posture (raised hands) was common in ancient prayer; the moral qualifiers ('holy hands, without anger') may be the primary emphasis rather than the posture itself.
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: This verse immediately precedes 1 Tim 2:11-12 (women not to teach). It has the same passage type, the same author, the same contextual unit. The commanded posture for male prayer (hands raised) is almost universally ignored. If 2:11-12 is binding, 2:8 is equally so. The selective application of one verse and not the other in the same pericope is the clearest example of hermeneutical inconsistency in the NT.

literary_context: 1 Cor 14:26-40 instructions on orderly worship including prophecy and tongues; the silence command comes at the end of this section.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Women commanded to be silent in church assemblies and ask questions of husbands at home.
interpretive_notes: Significant tension with 1 Cor 11:5 where women praying/prophesying is regulated but not prohibited. Some scholars treat vv.34-35 as a scribal gloss; others as restricting a specific kind of speech (disruptive questioning). Widely debated.
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: C4=N: 1 Cor 11:5 assumes women pray and prophesy in the assembly. These commands cannot both be universally binding without contradiction. The silence command likely addresses a specific disruptive practice (chattering, questioning during service). If treated as universal, it prohibits female singing, female testimony, and female prophecy — which most complementarian churches permit.

literary_context: 1 Tim 2:8-15 — worship instructions; Paul addresses women specifically after addressing men.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Women to learn in quietness; Paul does not permit women to teach or exercise authority over men.
interpretive_notes: Authenteō (translated 'authority') occurs only here in NT; its meaning is disputed (domineering vs. normal authority). Contextual debates: whether this addresses a specific Ephesian problem or states universal norms.
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: C1=P: v.13 appeals to Adam being formed first (creation order — the strongest evangelical argument for universality). C3=P: some scholars argue Paul is addressing a specific Ephesian problem (women promoting false teaching). C4=N: v.15 ("she will be saved through childbearing") is in the same pericope — if vv.11-12 are binding, v.15 demands equal weight. C5=N: systematic exclusion of women from teaching creates structural tension with mercy-inclusion commands (A10, A12).

literary_context: 1 Cor 14 — Paul regulates the exercise of spiritual gifts in corporate worship; climax of the tongues/prophecy discussion.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Detailed instructions: tongues only with interpreter, prophets in turns (2-3), others judge; orderly and edifying.
interpretive_notes: The underlying principle is intelligibility and edification; Paul does not abolish tongues/prophecy but orders them.
q2_notes: The orderly-worship principle is universally applied.

literary_context: Conclusion of 1 Cor 14 — summary of Paul's instructions on spiritual gifts in worship.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Eagerly desire prophecy; do not forbid tongues; do everything orderly.
interpretive_notes: A summary command; notably does not abolish tongues. The positive command to prophecy is often underweighted in cessationist readings.
q2_notes: C4=N: This command appears in the same chapter as 14:34-35. Churches that enforce women's silence (from this chapter) while forbidding tongues are selectively applying two commands from the same passage.

literary_context: 1 Tim 2:8-15 — worship conduct instructions; follows the command to men (v.8).
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: kosmios; aidōs; sophrosynē; ploke; chrysos; margarites
summary: Women to dress modestly with self-control, not with elaborate braided hair, gold, pearls, or expensive clothing.
interpretive_notes: The contrast is between costly, status-displaying ornamentation and modest/respectable dress. Whether specific items (braids, gold) are prohibited absolutely or only as status symbols is debated.
q2_typical_application: SELECTIVE (modesty principle retained; specific prohibitions ignored)
q2_notes: The modesty principle is affirmed but the specific prohibitions (braided hair, gold, pearls, costly attire) are universally ignored. Adjacent verse 1 Tim 2:11-12 is treated as binding — a hermeneutical asymmetry within the same passage.

literary_context: 1 Pet 3:1-6 — advice to wives; part of the household code; the dress instruction follows the submission command.
speaker: Peter (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: kosmos; emploke; peribolē; praus; hesychios pneuma
summary: True adornment is inward (gentle spirit) not outward (braided hair, gold, fine clothes).
interpretive_notes: The inner/outer contrast is the emphasis; most interpreters read this as redirecting values rather than issuing an absolute prohibition on hairstyling or jewelry.
q2_notes: Modesty principle applied without enforcing specific cultural prohibitions.

literary_context: 1 Cor 11:17-34 — Paul corrects the Corinthian Lord's Supper abuses; quotes the institution narrative received from the Lord.
speaker: Jesus (quoted by Paul)
summary: Institution of the Lord's Supper: 'Do this in remembrance of me' — eat the bread, drink the cup.
interpretive_notes: The most widely observed NT liturgical command; debated in terms of frequency ('as often as'), mode, and theological significance (symbol vs. real presence).
q2_notes: Dominical command; universally observed.

literary_context: 1 Cor 11:17-34 — Paul's corrective following the institution narrative; Corinthians were treating the Supper in a way that shamed the poor.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Each person to examine themselves before taking communion; eating unworthily brings judgment.
interpretive_notes: The 'unworthy manner' in context was the class-based abuse of the Supper (rich eating while poor went hungry). The self-examination command is the remedy Paul prescribes.

literary_context: James 5:13-18 — section on prayer, healing, and mutual confession; concludes the epistle.
speaker: James (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: astheneō; presbyteros; aleiphō; elaion; proseuchē pisteos
summary: Sick persons to call elders; elders to pray and anoint with oil in the Lord's name.
interpretive_notes: Roman Catholics treat this as Extreme Unction; most Protestants understand it as prayer for healing of the sick. The oil may have had medicinal connotation (cf. Luke 10:34).
q2_typical_application: SELECTIVELY APPLIED (prayer for sick affirmed; oil anointing varies widely)
q2_notes: The purpose (corporate prayer for healing) is universally affirmed, but the specific form (anointing with oil by elders) is practiced inconsistently.

literary_context: James 5:13-18 — continues from anointing command; mutual practice of confession and intercession.
speaker: James (apostolic letter)
summary: Christians commanded to confess sins to one another and pray for one another.
interpretive_notes: Roman Catholic tradition grounds auricular confession partly here; most Protestants emphasize the mutual and communal nature (to one another, not to a priest).
q2_typical_application: SELECTIVELY APPLIED (prayer affirmed; mutual confession often minimized)
q2_notes: Evangelicals universally affirm "pray for one another" but often minimize or privatize "confess your sins to one another." The passage treats them as a single unit; typical application separates them.

literary_context: Rom 16:1-23 — closing greetings and instructions; one of five NT occurrences of the holy-kiss command.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Greet one another with a holy kiss.
interpretive_notes: Repeated five times across the NT epistles. Most modern traditions substitute a handshake or embrace; some Orthodox and Coptic communities retain a liturgical kiss practice.
q2_typical_application: IGNORED (form universally replaced; greeting principle retained)
q2_notes: One of five NT holy-kiss commands. The explicit form (kiss) is universally replaced with a handshake or embrace — a Purpose-Form transfer that is applied here but inconsistently acknowledged elsewhere.

literary_context: 1 Cor 16 — closing of the epistle; standard Pauline closing greeting.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Greet one another with a holy kiss.
interpretive_notes: One of five NT occurrences; formulaic closing instruction.
q2_notes: Second occurrence. See Rom 16:16.

literary_context: 1 Thess 5:12-28 — closing instructions; immediately follows commands on prophecy and discernment.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Greet all the brothers with a holy kiss.
interpretive_notes: One of five NT occurrences.
q2_notes: Third occurrence — in the same letter as 1 Thess 5:17 (pray without ceasing) which is universally applied.

literary_context: 1 Cor 6:1-11 — Paul rebukes believers taking disputes to pagan courts; argues for internal church resolution.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Christians should not take disputes against fellow believers before secular courts; settle them within the church.
interpretive_notes: Paul's rhetorical escalation (will judge the world, judge angels) grounds the prohibition in eschatological dignity. He does not address disputes with unbelievers.
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: Explicit command form; addresses a universal situation (disputes between believers); no cultural specificity. C4=N: rarely enforced — evangelical churches routinely allow and participate in civil litigation between members. By any consistent criterion that makes 1 Tim 2:11-12 binding, this command should be equally binding.

literary_context: 2 Cor 6:14-7:1 — a sharp exhortation to separation; some scholars view this as a digression; others as the climax of Paul's appeal for reconciliation.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
ot_antecedent: Deut 22:10 (do not yoke ox and donkey together); Lev 19:19
summary: Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers.
interpretive_notes: Widely applied to marriage with non-Christians (cf. 1 Cor 7:39: 'marry only in the Lord'), but the original context may be broader — pagan cultic meals or business partnerships. Application to marriage is inferential.
q2_typical_application: SELECTIVE (applied to marriage; ignored in business)
q2_notes: Commonly applied exclusively to marriage despite the passage's broader context (the temple of God, 6:16). The narrowing of application to marriage while ignoring business partnerships is selective.

literary_context: 1 Cor 5 — Paul's instructions on the case of incest in the Corinthian church; the non-association command applies specifically to professing believers.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Do not associate with or even eat with a person who calls himself a brother but lives in ongoing sin.
interpretive_notes: Carefully qualified: the separation is only from professing Christians, not unbelievers. The list of sins is broader than the specific incest case.
q2_typical_application: SELECTIVE (applied to sexual immorality; greedy/slanderers less enforced)
q2_notes: CRITICAL CASE: Paul lists sexual immorality alongside greed (pleonektēs) and slander. Most evangelical churches pursue discipline for sexual immorality but not for documented greed or slander among members — a selectivity that directly contradicts Paul's list.

literary_context: Matt 18:1-35 — discourse on greatness and forgiveness; the church discipline procedure comes immediately before the parable of the unmerciful servant.
speaker: Jesus
summary: Jesus prescribes a three-stage process for church discipline: private confrontation, then two or three witnesses, then community judgment — with authority to bind and loose in heaven.
interpretive_notes: The goal throughout is restoration ('you have gained your brother'); exclusion is the last resort, not the first response.
q2_typical_application: AFFIRMED (but often applied punitively rather than restoratively)
q2_notes: Jesus intends a restorative process, but church discipline is often applied punitively — excommunication rather than reconciliation.

literary_context: 2 Thess 3:6-15 — Paul's command concerning idle members who were not working; part of his closing instructions.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Keep away from any believer living in idleness contrary to apostolic teaching.
interpretive_notes: Context is likely eschatological idleness: some stopped working because they believed the Lord's return was imminent.

literary_context: 2 Thess 3 — conclusion of the discipline passage on idleness.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Mark and avoid the disobedient person so they are ashamed, but treat them as a brother not an enemy.
interpretive_notes: The restorative framing ('warn as a brother') qualifies the withdrawal; this is discipline for correction, not rejection.

literary_context: Titus 3:9-11 — brief instruction on handling divisive people; follows warnings about controversies and genealogies.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: A divisive person should be warned once, then twice, then shunned.
interpretive_notes: Hairetikon (divisive person — root of 'heretic') refers here to someone who causes factions, not necessarily doctrinal error.

literary_context: Rom 16:17-20 — Paul's penultimate warning before final greetings.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
summary: Watch out for and avoid those who cause divisions and teach contrary to received doctrine.
interpretive_notes: The description ('smooth talk, flattery, deceiving the naive') suggests charismatic false teachers; the command is to avoid, not engage or debate.

literary_context: 1 Cor 7:39-40 — Paul's instruction on remarriage of widows; conclusion of the chapter on marriage.
speaker: Paul (apostolic letter)
gk_keywords: deomai; aner; eleuthera; monon en Kyriō
summary: A widow is free to remarry, but only to a believer ('in the Lord').
interpretive_notes: The 'only in the Lord' qualifier is the basis for evangelical teaching against marrying unbelievers (cf. 2 Cor 6:14 application).

literary_context: 2 John 7-11 — the elder warns against receiving travelling false teachers (those who deny the incarnation) into home-based churches.
speaker: The Elder (John)
summary: Do not welcome into your home or greet anyone who comes with a teaching other than Christ's.
